items.requirement: The employer must evaluate the workplace to determine if any spaces qualify as permit-required confined spaces (PRCS). A permit-required confined space is a space that: (1) is large enough and configured so an employee can bodily enter and perform assigned work; (2) has limited or restricted means for entry or exit; AND (3) is not designed for continuous employee occupancy; AND meets one or more of: contains or has the potential to contain a serious atmospheric hazard; contains a material with potential for engulfment; has an internal configuration that could trap or asphyxiate an entrant; contains any other recognized serious safety or health hazard.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written confined space inventory/survey document. Walk 10% of the facility (minimum 3 representative areas — processing areas, utility rooms, below-grade areas, material handling areas) and confirm the inventory captures all spaces meeting the PRCS definition. Look for commonly missed spaces: dust collector hoppers, conveyor pits, below-grade sumps, enclosed ductwork, storage bins, silos, and large tanks. Confirm the inventory is signed, dated, and updated when the facility layout changes.

items.requirement: Each identified permit-required confined space must be identified by posting a warning sign at or near the entry point. Signs must include wording such as: 'DANGER — PERMIT-REQUIRED CONFINED SPACE — AUTHORIZED ENTRANTS ONLY' (or equivalent language that conveys the hazard). If signage is not practical, another equally effective means of informing employees must be used.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 100% of identified PRCS locations and confirm warning signs are posted at or near each entry point. Signs must be visible before an employee could inadvertently enter the space. Document any identified PRCS that lacks a sign or where the sign is obscured, illegible, or outdated.

items.requirement: If the employer decides employees will not enter any confined space, the employer must take effective measures to prevent employee entry — not just post a sign. Effective measures include: welding or bolting covers shut, installing padlocked barriers, or other physical means that prevent unauthorized access.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether any spaces on the inventory are designated no-entry. If yes, physically inspect 100% of no-entry spaces and confirm physical prevention measures are in place (not just a sign or verbal instruction). Verify employees and contractors know the no-entry designation and understand they may not enter under any circumstances without a program revision.

items.requirement: When contractors perform work at this facility, the host employer must: (1) Inform the contractor that the workplace contains permit-required confined spaces. (2) Inform the contractor of the spaces' locations and the hazards present. (3) Inform the contractor of the employer's experience with the spaces. (4) Inform the contractor of any precautions or procedures implemented for protection. (5) Coordinate entry operations if both employer and contractor will be working in or near a PRCS simultaneously. (6) Debrief the contractor after entry operations to identify any hazards discovered.
items.evidence_protocol: Request documentation of contractor coordination for 10% of contractor jobs involving PRCS work at this facility in the past year (minimum 1 job). Confirm: the host shared the space inventory and known hazards before work began; there is a record of the pre-entry information exchange (sign-in sheet, pre-job meeting notes, or contractor safety form); and if both host and contractor worked in or near the same space simultaneously, a coordination protocol is documented.

items.requirement: An employer whose employees enter permit spaces must develop and implement a written PRCS entry program. The program must be made available for inspection by employees and their authorized representatives.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written PRCS program. Confirm: (1) it is site-specific, not a generic template; (2) it references the specific spaces at this facility; (3) it includes a current revision date; (4) it is physically accessible to employees and authorized employee representatives — not solely in a manager's office or a locked file. A printed copy posted near the entry or maintained in the work area meets this requirement.

items.requirement: The written PRCS program must include ALL 14 required elements per 29 CFR 1910.146(d)(3)-(d)(14). Missing any element is a citable deficiency. See the Evidence column checklist.
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — review the written PRCS program against each element. Mark each: Verified (complete), Partial (exists but incomplete), or Absent (not addressed at all).
□ (1)  Measures to prevent unauthorized entry
□ (2)  Identification and evaluation of hazards before entry
□ (3)  Safe entry procedures: acceptable entry conditions; isolation from energy/material sources; condition verification throughout entry; purging/inerting/flushing/ventilation to control atmospheric hazards; pedestrian/vehicle barriers
□ (4)  Equipment at no cost: testing instruments, ventilation, communications, PPE, lighting, ingress/egress, rescue gear
□ (5)  Atmospheric testing and monitoring before and during entry
□ (6)  At least one dedicated attendant stationed outside for the full duration of every authorized entry
□ (7)  Multi-space attendant protocol if one attendant monitors simultaneous entries
□ (8)  Designated roles with responsibilities: authorized entrants, attendants, entry supervisors, rescuers
□ (9)  Rescue and emergency services — on-site trained team or identified external service
□ (10) Permit system: preparation, issuance, use, and cancellation
□ (11) Multi-employer coordination when >1 employer works in or near the space simultaneously
□ (12) Entry conclusion and permit cancellation procedures
□ (13) Pre-entry hazard re-evaluation when conditions may have changed
□ (14) Annual program review using canceled permits as input
Elements (6), (9), and (14) are most frequently missing — verify these first.

items.requirement: The employer must provide and maintain all equipment necessary for safe PRCS entry at no cost to the employee. Required equipment includes: (1) Testing and monitoring equipment for atmospheric hazards (calibrated multi-gas meter for O2, LEL, CO, and H2S at minimum); (2) Ventilating equipment sufficient to obtain acceptable entry conditions; (3) Communications equipment; (4) Personal protective equipment; (5) Lighting equipment to enable employees to see well enough to work safely and exit quickly in an emergency; (6) Barriers and shields; (7) Equipment for safe ingress and egress; (8) Rescue and emergency equipment.
items.evidence_protocol: Physically inspect the confined space entry equipment station. Verify: (1) Multi-gas meter is present, powered on, and the calibration date on the tag or in the log is current (most meters require bump testing before each use and full calibration per manufacturer schedule — typically every 6 months); (2) Ventilation blower is present and functional; (3) Communications device (radio or phone) is present and working; (4) Rescue retrieval equipment (tripod, winch, or davit) is present and in serviceable condition; (5) Full-body harness and retrieval lifeline are present; (6) Lighting is available. Sample 10% of confined space entry equipment kits at this facility (minimum 1 full kit inspection).

items.requirement: Atmospheric monitoring must be conducted: (1) Before entry authorization is granted — to test for the presence of atmospheric hazards. (2) As needed during entry — to ensure acceptable entry conditions are maintained for the duration of the entry. Testing must be conducted by or under the direction of a qualified person using properly calibrated instruments. The atmosphere must be tested for: oxygen content (acceptable range: 19.5% to 23.5%); flammable gases and vapors (must be less than 10% of LFL); potential toxic air contaminants (below applicable PEL).
items.evidence_protocol: Pull 10% of canceled entry permits from the past 12 months (minimum 5 permits). For each permit: confirm pre-entry atmospheric test results are recorded with the actual readings (not just a checkmark), the name of the person who performed the test, and the instrument used. Confirm readings were within acceptable ranges before entry was authorized. Confirm that any permit where continuous monitoring was required shows monitoring notes during the entry. Any permit with blank atmospheric test fields is a deficiency.

items.requirement: An employer may use an alternate procedure (continuous forced-air ventilation without a full permit) ONLY when ALL of the following conditions are met: (1) The only hazard in the space is an actual or potential hazardous atmosphere; (2) The hazardous atmosphere can be controlled by continuous forced-air ventilation alone; (3) The employer can demonstrate that entry can be made safely; (4) The written program describes the means, procedures, and practices used; (5) The atmosphere is tested before any employee enters to confirm the ventilation has worked; (6) Atmospheric testing continues as necessary.
items.evidence_protocol: For any spaces designated as alternate-entry (non-permit entry with ventilation), confirm the written program documents specifically that: (1) the space has been evaluated and the ONLY hazard is atmospheric; (2) ventilation controls the hazard; (3) pre-entry and continuous atmospheric monitoring is performed. Walk 10% of alternate-entry spaces (minimum 1) and confirm ventilation equipment is in place and functional. Any space with an engulfment hazard, converging walls, or uncontrolled energy cannot qualify for alternate entry.

items.requirement: When an alternate entry procedure is used, the employer must: (1) Test the atmosphere within the space before any employee enters to demonstrate the ventilation has worked. (2) Continue atmospheric testing as often as necessary to ensure acceptable conditions exist throughout the entry. (3) If a hazardous atmosphere is detected during entry, have each employee leave the space immediately and investigate, evaluate, and remedy the hazardous atmosphere before re-entering.
items.evidence_protocol: Review 10% of alternate-entry records (minimum 2). Confirm: pre-entry atmospheric readings are recorded with the actual values; continuous monitoring requirements are documented; and there is a documented procedure (or training record showing employees know) to evacuate immediately if conditions change.

items.requirement: A permit-required confined space may be reclassified as a non-permit-required confined space ONLY when: (1) the space contains no actual or potential serious safety or health hazards; AND (2) the employer documents that the hazardous characteristics have been permanently eliminated (not just controlled). The documentation must be certified and made available to employees.
items.evidence_protocol: If any spaces have been reclassified from PRCS to non-permit status, request the written reclassification documentation for 100% of reclassified spaces. Confirm: the certification specifically states how hazards were eliminated (not merely controlled); it is signed by a qualified person; and it identifies the space by location. Re-entry into a reclassified space that later shows a hazardous atmosphere requires the space to be immediately evacuated and re-evaluated.

items.requirement: Before entry is authorized, the employer must prepare and issue an entry permit containing ALL 15 required components per 29 CFR 1910.146(f). A permit missing any required component is deficient. See Evidence column checklist.
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — for each permit sampled (10% of canceled permits, minimum 5), verify every component is present and completed (not blank).
□ (1)  The permit space to be entered (identified by location)
□ (2)  Purpose of the entry
□ (3)  Date and authorized duration of the entry permit
□ (4)  Authorized entrants — by name or a means (roster/badge system) to identify all entrants quickly
□ (5)  Current attendant(s) — by name
□ (6)  Entry supervisor — identified with signature or initials BEFORE entry
□ (7)  Hazards of the permit space
□ (8)  Measures to isolate the space and eliminate or control hazards
□ (9)  Acceptable entry conditions (specific ranges: O2 19.5-23.5%, LEL <10%, CO <35 ppm, etc.)
□ (10) Results of initial and periodic atmospheric tests — actual readings, tester name, instrument used
□ (11) Rescue and emergency services — specific contact information (phone number, not just 'call 911')
□ (12) Communication procedures between entrants and attendant
□ (13) Required equipment: PPE, testing equipment, communications, alarm, rescue equipment, lighting
□ (14) Any other information necessary to ensure employee safety
□ (15) Any additional permits (e.g., hot work permit) for work in the space
Document the % of sampled permits with all 15 components complete.

items.requirement: The permit must be properly used before and during entry: (1) Signed by the entry supervisor who verifies that all required entries on the permit have been made; (2) Made available to authorized entrants through posting at the permit space entrance or another equally effective means; (3) Actively used by the attendant to track authorized entrants; (4) Retained after operations are completed and the permit is canceled.
items.evidence_protocol: For 10% of the most recent entries (minimum 3), confirm: the permit bears the entry supervisor's initials or signature from before entry began; permits were posted at the entry point (ask whether this is standard practice or review a photo/checklist confirming it); the attendant's duty to maintain a count of entrants is reflected on or with the permit. Ask the entry supervisor to walk through how they authorize an entry — not after-the-fact signature.

items.requirement: Entry must be terminated and the permit canceled under two conditions: (1) When the work covered by the permit has been completed; OR (2) When a prohibited condition arises — any condition not allowed by the permit, including an atmospheric reading outside acceptable parameters, an equipment failure, or an unexpected hazard. All authorized entrants must immediately evacuate when entry is terminated due to a prohibited condition.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm 100% of retained permits are marked as canceled/completed. Ask the entry supervisor: 'If you discovered during entry that the O2 level dropped to 18% — what would you do?' The correct answer is immediate evacuation, termination of the permit, and re-evaluation before re-entry. Confirm this is reflected in the written program.

items.requirement: Canceled entry permits must be retained for at least 1 year following each entry. Within 1 year of each entry, the employer must review the canceled permits for that entry and use them to revise the permit program as needed to ensure that employees are protected from permit space hazards.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm canceled permits are filed and accessible for the past 12 months (100% of the prior year's permits should be on file). Ask when the most recent annual program review was completed and request the documentation. Confirm the review references specific findings from prior permits.

items.requirement: Training must be provided to each employee whose work is regulated by the PRCS standard. Training must be provided: (1) Before the employee is first assigned duties under the standard; (2) Before there is a change in assigned duties; (3) Whenever there is a change in permit space operations that presents a hazard that employees have not been trained for; (4) Whenever the employer has reason to believe employees do not have the required understanding, knowledge, or skills. Training must establish employee proficiency in duties assigned and must be specific to the role (authorized entrant, attendant, entry supervisor, rescue).
items.evidence_protocol: Request training records for 100% of currently designated authorized entrants, attendants, and entry supervisors. Confirm: initial training was completed before first assignment; training is role-specific (separate content for entrants vs. attendants vs. supervisors); and the training record documents the specific spaces and hazards covered. Note whether training is refreshed when new spaces are added or hazard profiles change.

items.requirement: Authorized entrants must be able to: (1) Know the hazards that may be faced during entry, including information on the mode, signs or symptoms, and consequences of the exposure; (2) Properly use equipment; (3) Communicate with the attendant as necessary; (4) Alert the attendant whenever the entrant recognizes any warning sign or symptom of exposure or a prohibited condition; (5) Exit from the permit space as quickly as possible whenever ordered by the attendant or entry supervisor, an evacuation alarm is activated, or they recognize a warning sign, symptom, or prohibited condition.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of authorized entrants (minimum 3). Ask each entrant: 'What hazards are you exposed to in the confined spaces you enter at this facility?' 'What atmospheric conditions would cause you to leave immediately?' 'What are three ways you communicate with the attendant?' 'If the attendant said leave — what do you do?' Grade responses against the five required knowledge areas above.

items.requirement: Attendants must be able to: (1) Know the hazards that may be faced during entry; (2) Know the behavioral effects of hazard exposure; (3) Continuously maintain an accurate count of authorized entrants in the permit space; (4) Remain outside the permit space during entry operations and NOT enter the space under any circumstances unless a relieving attendant has taken over; (5) Communicate with entrants as necessary; (6) Monitor activities inside and outside the space to determine whether it is safe for entrants to remain; (7) Order evacuation when a prohibited condition occurs, an entrant shows behavioral effects, an emergency arises, or the attendant cannot perform duties; (8) Summon rescue services without entering the space; (9) Prevent unauthorized entry; (10) Perform non-entry rescues per the rescue procedure.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of designated attendants (minimum 3). Ask: 'Can you ever enter the space yourself?' (Answer: NO, unless formally relieved by another attendant) 'What do you do if you lose communication with an entrant?' 'How do you summon rescue?' 'Can you leave your post to help someone?' (Answer: NO — they must call for relief). Pay particular attention to whether attendants understand the prohibition on entry and the distinction between their role and an authorized entrant's role.

items.requirement: Entry supervisors must be able to: (1) Know the hazards that may be faced; (2) Verify by checking all entries on the permit that all tests, procedures, and equipment are in place before endorsing and authorizing entry; (3) Terminate entry and cancel the permit when required; (4) Verify that rescue services are available; (5) Remove unauthorized individuals; (6) Determine whenever responsibility for a permit space entry operation is transferred to a new supervisor, and when conditions change, whether it is still safe to enter.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of entry supervisors (minimum 2). Ask: 'Walk me through what you check before you authorize an entry.' Confirm they verify atmospheric readings, equipment availability, team assignments, and rescue contacts — not just sign the permit. Ask: 'If an entrant called you from inside the space and said they smelled something unusual — what is your immediate action?' (Expected: order immediate evacuation, terminate the permit, investigate).

items.requirement: The employer must select a rescue service capable of responding to a rescue summons in a timely manner, considering the hazards identified and the type of permit space operations at this facility. If an off-site emergency responder (fire department) is used, the employer must: (1) Inform the responder of the hazards; (2) Provide the responder with access to the facility for pre-entry rescue planning; (3) Evaluate the service's ability to respond effectively.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether rescue services are internal (on-site trained team) or external (fire department or contract rescue). For external rescue: request documentation that the service was informed of the specific hazards present (copies of SDSs, hazard descriptions, space configurations shared with the responder); confirm the service has visited the facility for pre-planning (emergency plan pre-entry briefing, drill, or site walk); and confirm the estimated response time is acceptable for the hazard level in each PRCS.

items.requirement: If the employer uses on-site employees for rescue, those rescue employees must: (1) Be provided with and trained in the use of PPE and rescue equipment required for the space, at no cost to the employee; (2) Be trained to perform assigned rescue duties, including first aid and CPR; (3) Practice making permit space rescues at least once every 12 months, using a representative permit space (actual space or mockup with similar hazards and entry/exit conditions).
items.evidence_protocol: If an internal rescue team is used: request the training records for 100% of designated rescue team members. Confirm: PPE and rescue equipment training is documented; current CPR and first aid certification is on file for each member; and an annual rescue practice drill using an actual or representative space was conducted within the past 12 months — with documentation of the drill (date, participants, space used, lessons learned).

items.requirement: Retrieval equipment must be used whenever an authorized entrant enters a permit space, unless the retrieval equipment would increase the overall risk of entry or would not contribute to the rescue. Retrieval systems must include: (1) Each authorized entrant uses a chest or full body harness with a retrieval line attached to the center ring or wrist straps; (2) The other end of the retrieval line is attached to a mechanical device or fixed point outside the space; (3) A mechanical device capable of retrieving the entrant from vertical entry spaces more than 5 feet deep.
items.evidence_protocol: Physically inspect the retrieval system used for permit space entries at this facility. Confirm: full-body harnesses are present and sized for the workforce; retrieval lines are connected to a mechanical retrieval device (tripod and winch) positioned outside the space; the attendant can operate the system from their station without entering the space; the device is rated for the load. If retrieval equipment is not used for certain spaces, confirm written documentation justifying the exception (e.g., the configuration of the space makes retrieval equipment more hazardous than beneficial).

items.requirement: Pertinent safety data sheets (SDSs) or other written information regarding the hazards of chemicals in the permit space must be made available to the medical facility treating exposed entrants at the time of emergency response.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that a packet of SDSs or a chemical hazard summary is kept with or near the entry equipment kit so it can be sent with any injured entrant to the hospital. Ask the entry supervisor: 'If an entrant needed to be transported to the ER after a chemical exposure in one of your permit spaces — how would the hospital know what they were exposed to?' The process should not require someone to search for SDSs after an emergency has begun.

items.requirement: The PRCS program must be reviewed within 1 year after each entry and whenever the employer has reason to believe the measures in the program may not protect employees adequately. The review must use canceled entry permits as input. If deficiencies are identified, the program must be revised before employees next enter the permit space.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the most recent annual PRCS program review document. Confirm: it was completed within 12 months of the most recent entries; it references specific findings from the canceled permits reviewed (e.g., atmospheric readings that were close to limits, equipment issues, permit completion rate); and any identified deficiencies resulted in documented program revisions. If no annual review documentation exists, this is a significant finding.

items.requirement: The employer must assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present that necessitate PPE. Where required, PPE must be provided, maintained in a safe and reliable condition, and used by employees. PPE must not be defective or damaged.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk all work areas (minimum 5 representative areas including production floor, maintenance, and shipping). Observe employees performing tasks requiring PPE. For each area, confirm: appropriate PPE is in use and matches the hazard level; PPE is in serviceable condition — no cracked safety glasses, torn gloves, or hard hats with cracks or deep gouges; and a replacement process exists for damaged PPE.

items.requirement: The employer must pay for required PPE — with the following exceptions where employees may be required to provide their own: (1) non-specialty safety-toe footwear (if worn off the job), (2) non-specialty prescription safety eyewear (if worn off the job), (3) logging boots, (4) ordinary clothing, skin creams, or other items used solely for protection from weather.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the employer provides all required PPE at no cost. Interview 10% of employees in PPE-required areas (minimum 5). Ask whether they have ever been asked to purchase their own safety glasses, hard hats, gloves, or other required PPE.

items.requirement: Where employees provide their own PPE, the employer must ensure the equipment is adequate to protect the employee. The employer must be responsible for the adequacy of such equipment, including maintenance and sanitation.
items.evidence_protocol: If any employees use personal PPE (e.g., prescription safety glasses or personal steel-toed boots), confirm the employer has a documented approval process to verify the equipment meets the required protection standard. Confirm the employer's responsibility for adequacy is addressed in the written PPE program.

items.requirement: The employer must assess the workplace to determine PPE needs and certify that the assessment was performed. The written hazard assessment certification must identify: (1) the workplace evaluated; (2) the name of the person certifying the evaluation was performed; (3) the date of the hazard assessment; and (4) a statement that the document identifies it as a certification of hazard assessment.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written PPE hazard assessment/certification. Confirm all four required elements are present. Review the assessment content: confirm it covers all significant tasks performed at this facility (not just production — include maintenance, housekeeping, shipping/receiving, and lab if applicable) and that PPE selections are matched to specific hazards identified for each task or area. Confirm the assessment was reviewed following any significant process change in the past 12 months.

items.requirement: The employer must provide training to each employee required to use PPE. Training must cover: (1) when PPE is necessary; (2) what PPE is necessary; (3) how to properly don (put on), doff (remove), adjust, and wear PPE; (4) limitations of PPE; (5) proper care, maintenance, useful life, and disposal of PPE. Employees who demonstrate inadequate understanding must be retrained.
items.evidence_protocol: Request PPE training records for 10% of employees in PPE-required roles (minimum 10 employees). Confirm training covers all five content areas above. Interview 10% of employees using PPE (minimum 3). Ask: 'How do you inspect your safety glasses before putting them on?' 'When would you replace your hard hat?' 'What does your PPE not protect you from?' Grade answers against the required training content.

items.requirement: Each affected employee must use appropriate eye or face protection when exposed to eye or face hazards. Hazard types requiring protection include: (1) flying particles — safety glasses with side shields or goggles; (2) molten metal — goggles or face shield; (3) liquid chemicals — chemical splash goggles; (4) acid/caustic liquids — splash goggles with face shield; (5) chemical gases/vapors — chemical splash goggles; (6) injurious light radiation — filter lenses with appropriate shade number; (7) lasers — laser safety eyewear. Employees who wear prescription lenses must wear either prescription safety eyewear or safety eyewear designed to fit over their corrective lenses.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk work areas where eye hazards exist (minimum 5 areas). Confirm: the correct protection type is matched to the specific hazard; side shields are present on safety glasses used where flying particle hazards exist; chemical splash goggles are used for chemical handling (not just safety glasses, which do not seal against liquid splash); face shields are used in conjunction with goggles (not as a replacement) for splash hazards. Note whether prescription-lens employees have appropriate safety eyewear.

items.requirement: Protective eye and face devices must meet the requirements of ANSI/ISEA Z87.1 — American National Standard Practice for Occupational and Educational Eye and Face Protection. All eye and face protection devices must be marked by the manufacturer with the appropriate ANSI designation.
items.evidence_protocol: Review issued safety glasses, goggles, and face shields in use. Confirm each device is marked 'Z87' or 'Z87+' (the '+' designation indicates high-impact protection). Reject any unmarked or consumer-grade eyewear used in industrial tasks.

items.requirement: Filter lenses and plates must meet ANSI requirements. For protection against radiant energy (welding, brazing, cutting), lenses must have a shade number appropriate for the specific process. Shade number guidance: Shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) at 10-500 amps = Shade 10-14; MIG/MAG welding = Shade 10-13; gas welding/brazing = Shade 4-8; oxygen cutting = Shade 3-6; plasma arc welding = Shade 6-14.
items.evidence_protocol: If welding, cutting, or brazing occurs at this facility: identify the specific processes and confirm the shade number of filter lenses in use for each process. Use the shade number guide above. If shades are inadequate (too low), document as a deficiency. Confirm auto-darkening helmets used in welding applications are ANSI Z87.1 rated.

items.requirement: Each affected employee must wear a protective helmet when working in areas posing a risk of head injury from: (1) falling or flying objects; (2) bumping the head against fixed objects (low clearance, protruding equipment); (3) electrical shock/burns. Hard hats must be worn with the bill facing forward unless the manufacturer specifically certifies the helmet for reverse use while maintaining the required protection class.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of work areas identified in the PPE hazard assessment as requiring head protection (minimum 5 areas). Confirm: hard hats are worn by all employees, visitors, and contractors in required areas; no hard hats are worn backwards unless the manufacturer's certification for reverse use is confirmed; and hard hats are not suspended from equipment or stored in direct sunlight for extended periods (UV degrades the shell).

items.requirement: Head protection must meet ANSI/ISEA Z89.1 criteria. Hard hats are classified by type and class: Type I protects the top of the head; Type II protects the top and sides. Classes: Class E (electrical, rated to 20,000 volts); Class G (general, rated to 2,200 volts); Class C (conductive, no electrical protection). Facilities with electrical hazards must use Class E helmets in electrical work areas.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of hard hats in use (minimum 10 hard hats). Check the inside of each shell for: ANSI Z89.1 marking; the protection class (E, G, or C); the manufacture date (most manufacturers recommend retirement at 2-5 years from manufacture date and 2-5 years from first use, whichever comes first). Confirm the class used in electrical work areas is Class E, not Class G or C.

items.requirement: Protective footwear must be worn by employees working in areas where there is a danger of: (1) foot injuries from falling or rolling objects; (2) foot injuries from objects piercing the sole; (3) foot injuries from electrical hazards. Safety footwear selection must match the hazard type (impact, compression, metatarsal, electrical hazard, puncture resistance).
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of work areas requiring safety footwear (minimum 5 areas). Confirm all employees, visitors, and contractors in those areas are wearing safety-toe footwear. Ask 10% of employees (minimum 5): 'How do you know your boots provide the right protection for the hazards here?' Confirm the PPE hazard assessment specifies the protection requirements by area.

items.requirement: Protective footwear must meet ASTM F2412 (test methods) and ASTM F2413 (performance requirements), or the previous ANSI Z41 standard for footwear procured before the ASTM standard's adoption. Required markings on the label: ASTM designation; protection class (I/75 for impact, C/75 for compression, EH for electrical hazard, PR for puncture resistant, MT/75 for metatarsal).
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect footwear of 10% of employees in required areas (minimum 5 employees). Ask employees to check the label inside their boot for the ASTM F2413 designation and confirm the protection class matches the hazard requirements in the PPE hazard assessment. Consumer-grade work boots without ASTM markings do not meet the standard.

items.requirement: Insulating blankets, matting, covers, line hoses, gloves, and sleeves made of rubber must meet ASTM design requirements. Each piece must be marked with the voltage class for which it provides protection. Classes: 00 (max use voltage 500V AC), 0 (1000V), 1 (7,500V), 2 (17,000V), 3 (26,500V), 4 (36,000V). Other types of electrical protective equipment must also meet applicable ASTM standards.
items.evidence_protocol: If electrically rated rubber PPE is in use at this facility: inspect 10% of rubber insulating gloves, blankets, and sleeves (minimum 3 pieces). Confirm: voltage class markings are present and match the voltage of equipment worked on; no visible damage (cracks, cuts, punctures, swelling) is present; and gloves are stored rolled (not folded) in their protective bags.

items.requirement: Electrical protective equipment must be maintained in a safe, reliable condition. Rubber insulating equipment must be inspected before each day's use and immediately following an incident that may have caused damage. Rubber insulating equipment must be tested at regular intervals as follows: gloves and sleeves — every 6 months; blankets, covers, and line hoses — every 12 months. Equipment that fails inspection or testing must be immediately removed from service.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the electrical PPE test log for 100% of rubber insulating PPE currently in service. Confirm: test dates are within the required intervals (gloves/sleeves within 6 months; blankets/covers/line hoses within 12 months); equipment with expired tests has been removed from service; and the log identifies each piece of equipment individually. Pull out a set of gloves — check the date stamp on the test tag inside the bag.

items.requirement: Employers must select and require employees to use appropriate hand protection when hands are exposed to: (1) skin absorption of harmful substances; (2) severe cuts or lacerations; (3) severe abrasions; (4) punctures; (5) chemical burns; (6) thermal burns; (7) harmful temperature extremes. Glove selection must be based on the specific chemical or physical hazard — a single glove material does not protect against all chemicals.
items.evidence_protocol: Review the PPE hazard assessment for glove requirements. Walk 10% of chemical handling, sharp material, and heat work areas (minimum 5 areas). For each chemical task: confirm the glove material is appropriate for the specific chemical (consult the SDS glove recommendation in Section 8); nitrile gloves do not protect against all solvents; latex gloves do not protect against all acids; thin surgical-style gloves do not protect against cuts. Interview 10% of employees using gloves (minimum 5): ask what hazard their gloves protect against and whether they have ever experienced a skin exposure while wearing gloves (which may indicate wrong glove type).

items.requirement: Where respirators are necessary or required, a written respiratory protection program must be implemented containing ALL 9 required elements per 29 CFR 1910.134(c)(1). See Evidence column checklist.
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — review the written respiratory protection program against each required element. Mark each: Verified, Partial, or Absent.
□ (1)  Procedures for selecting respirators for use in the workplace
□ (2)  Medical evaluations of employees required to use respirators
□ (3)  Fit testing procedures for tight-fitting respirators
□ (4)  Procedures for proper use in routine and reasonably foreseeable emergency situations
□ (5)  Procedures and schedules for cleaning, disinfecting, storing, inspecting, repairing, discarding, and maintaining respirators
□ (6)  Procedures to ensure adequate air quality, quantity, and flow for atmosphere-supplying respirators
□ (7)  Training on the respiratory hazards to which employees may be exposed (routine and emergency)
□ (8)  Training on proper use, limitations, donning/doffing, and maintenance of respirators
□ (9)  Procedures for regularly evaluating the effectiveness of the program
Also confirm: program administrator is identified by name and has documented qualifications. If no respirator use occurs, confirm that a written evaluation exists determining no respirator is required.

items.requirement: Respirators, training, and medical evaluations must be provided at no cost to employees required to use them.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the employer provides respirators, training, and medical evaluations at no cost. Interview 10% of respirator-using employees (minimum 3): ask whether they were charged for any respirator-related cost (medical exam, fit test, or equipment).

items.requirement: A medical evaluation must be completed before an employee is required to use a respirator, and before fit testing. The physician or licensed health care professional (PLHCP) must determine whether the employee is medically able to use the respirator. The medical questionnaire must be administered confidentially and results must not be shared with the employer without the employee's permission. Additional evaluations must be provided if: employee reports signs or symptoms related to respirator use; PLHCP, supervisor, or program administrator recommends it; information from the respirator program indicates a need; or fit testing shows difficulty using the respirator.
items.evidence_protocol: Request medical evaluation records for 10% of respirator-using employees (minimum 5). Confirm: (1) initial evaluation was completed before first use — not concurrently with or after fit testing; (2) medical questionnaires were sent directly to the PLHCP (not reviewed by the supervisor or HR before submission); (3) the PLHCP issued a written recommendation letter confirming fitness for respirator use; and (4) employees who reported symptoms or experienced difficulty during fit testing received follow-up evaluations.

items.requirement: Before using a tight-fitting facepiece respirator, employees must pass a fit test with the same make, model, style, and size of respirator to be used on the job. Fit testing must be repeated: annually; when employees report changes in physical condition that could affect fit (significant weight gain/loss, dental work, facial scarring); and when the employer issues a different respirator facepiece. Both qualitative (QLFT) and quantitative (QNFT) fit test methods are acceptable for most respirators; QNFT is required for certain high-hazard respirator classes.
items.evidence_protocol: Request fit test records for 10% of tight-fitting respirator users (minimum 5). Confirm: (1) fit test was completed before first use — not during training or orientation; (2) the respirator model recorded on the fit test record matches exactly the model currently issued to the employee; (3) fit test was repeated within the past 12 months; (4) the fit test method used (QLFT or QNFT) is appropriate for the respirator class. Cross-check a sample of fit test records against the current respirator inventory to confirm the employee is using the same model tested.

items.requirement: Procedures must be established and implemented to prevent facepiece seal leakage. Specifically: (1) No tight-fitting respirator shall be worn by employees who have facial hair that comes between the sealing surface of the facepiece and the face, or that interferes with valve function. (2) No tight-fitting respirator shall be worn by employees with any other condition that prevents a good face seal. (3) Each employee who wears a tight-fitting respirator must perform a user seal check immediately before each use using the positive and/or negative pressure check method.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of areas where tight-fitting respirators are used (minimum 3 areas). Observe whether any employees wearing tight-fitting respirators have beards, goatees, or stubble in the sealing zone. Ask the safety manager how the no-facial-hair rule is enforced and communicated. Ask a respirator-wearing employee to demonstrate their user seal check before donning — confirm they use both the positive and negative pressure method or the manufacturer's recommended method.

items.requirement: Respirators must be properly maintained: (1) Cleaned and disinfected as often as necessary to be maintained in a sanitary condition, and before being worn by a different person; (2) Stored in a clean, sanitary location to protect from damage, contamination, dust, sunlight, extreme temperature, excessive moisture, and damaging chemicals; (3) Inspected before each use and during cleaning, including checking respirator function, facepiece, head straps, valves, connecting tube, cartridges, canisters or filters, and any other applicable component with reference to the manufacturer's recommendations; (4) Defective respirators removed from service and discarded or repaired.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of respirators currently in storage or in use (minimum 5 respirators). For each: confirm the facepiece is clean and free of cracking or discoloration; straps are intact; exhalation valve is not damaged; cartridges/filters are within their service life (confirm the change schedule is documented and followed). Inspect storage conditions — respirators should be individually bagged and stored away from chemicals, heat, and UV light. Ask whether a respirator discovered to be defective during a pre-use inspection has ever been taken out of service and how that was documented.

items.requirement: Training must be provided to each employee required to use a respirator. Training must occur before use and annually thereafter (or more frequently if warranted). Training must be understandable to the employee and must cover: (1) why the respirator is necessary and how improper fit, use, or maintenance can compromise protection; (2) limitations and capabilities of the respirator; (3) effective use in emergency situations; (4) how to inspect, put on, remove, and check the seals; (5) maintenance and storage procedures; (6) medical signs and symptoms that may limit or prevent effective use.
items.evidence_protocol: Request respirator training records for 10% of current respirator users (minimum 5). Confirm initial training before first use and annual retraining on file. Confirm training records show completion of all six content areas above (or confirm the training curriculum covers them). Interview 10% of respirator users (minimum 3): ask what hazard the respirator protects them from, when they would not use a respirator even if one were available (e.g., immediately dangerous to life and health atmosphere — should evacuate, not use a filter respirator), and how they store their respirator at end of shift.

items.requirement: The program administrator must conduct periodic workplace evaluations to ensure the program is being properly implemented and to consult with employees to identify problems. Evaluations must include: (1) conducting workplace and respirator use observations; (2) reviewing employee reports of problems; (3) identifying program deficiencies; and (4) correcting deficiencies before they affect employee health.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the most recent respiratory protection program evaluation records. Confirm: observations were conducted in the work area (not just a review of paperwork); employee feedback was solicited; and any identified deficiencies were addressed. Ask the program administrator when they last walked the areas where respirators are used and what they observed.

items.requirement: All personal fall protection systems must meet general requirements: (1) Be designed, constructed, and installed per the applicable ANSI/ASSE standard; (2) Have connectors (snap hooks, carabiners, D-rings) that are corrosion-resistant and load-rated; (3) Have locking-type snap hooks to prevent rollout; (4) Self-retracting lifelines must meet ANSI/ASSE Z359.14; (5) Full-body harnesses must meet ANSI/ASSE Z359.11; (6) Equipment must be inspected before each use and removed from service if defective; (7) Equipment must be retired after a fall arrest event.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of fall protection equipment in inventory (minimum 5 harnesses and 5 lanyards/SRLs). For each harness: confirm ANSI Z359.11 marking; check the inspection date in the annual inspection log; look for cuts, tears, fraying, worn stitching, deformed D-rings, or cracked hardware. For SRLs: confirm ANSI Z359.14 marking; pull the lifeline out 12 inches and confirm it retracts under tension. Ask whether any harness or SRL has been involved in a fall arrest event — those must be removed from service immediately.

items.requirement: Personal fall arrest systems must: (1) Limit maximum arresting force on the employee to 1,800 lbs when used with a body harness; (2) Be rigged such that the employee can fall no more than 6 feet, nor contact any lower level; (3) Bring an employee to a complete stop and limit maximum deceleration distance to 3.5 feet; (4) Have sufficient strength to withstand twice the potential impact load or at least 5,000 lbs per attached employee.
items.evidence_protocol: For areas where fall arrest systems are in use (elevated platforms, roof access, elevated maintenance areas): confirm the fall clearance calculation has been performed for each anchor point (total fall clearance = free fall + deceleration distance + worker height + safety factor — must not contact the lower level). Confirm anchor points or certified anchorage connectors are rated to 5,000 lbs per employee. Sample 10% of fall arrest anchor points in use (minimum 3).

items.requirement: Positioning systems must be rigged such that an employee cannot free-fall more than 2 feet. Positioning systems must be secured to an anchorage capable of supporting at least twice the potential impact load or at least 3,000 lbs, whichever is greater.
items.evidence_protocol: If positioning systems (body belts with positioning lanyards) are used for work positioning on structures, poles, or elevated work surfaces: confirm the lanyard length limits free fall to 2 feet and the anchor meets the 3,000-lb minimum. Note that body belts are only permitted for positioning — not for fall arrest.

items.requirement: All places of employment, passageways, storerooms, and service rooms must be kept in a clean, orderly, and sanitary condition. Floor surfaces must be kept clean and as dry as possible. Drainage must be maintained for wet process areas, and false floors, platforms, mats, or other dry-standing places must be provided where workers stand in wet conditions.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of the facility floor area (minimum 5 representative areas including production, maintenance, storage, and restroom/locker areas). Document: (1) any accumulations of debris, scrap, or product residue on walking surfaces; (2) wet areas without adequate drainage or dry-standing places; (3) oil or liquid spills not cleaned up; (4) any areas where material is stored in passageways or emergency egress paths. Compare findings to housekeeping inspection records to assess whether the inspection program is catching these conditions.

items.requirement: Each walking-working surface must be able to support the maximum intended load placed upon it. Loads must not exceed the surface's design load capacity.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of elevated platforms, mezzanines, catwalks, and storage areas (minimum 3 locations). Confirm: posted load capacity signs are present where maximum loads could be approached or exceeded; ask whether any process or storage configuration changes since the last inspection could affect load distribution. If any elevated surface shows visible signs of deflection, cracking, or corrosion, document for immediate engineering review.

items.requirement: The employer must provide safe means of access and egress to and from each elevated walking-working surface, and must ensure that employees use those means.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of elevated work areas (minimum 5). Confirm: a proper access means (fixed ladder, stair, ramp) is present and accessible; no improvised climbing methods are in use (stacked pallets, barrels, equipment used as steps). Ask employees how they access elevated equipment for routine maintenance — confirm the intended access route is practical and actually being used.

items.requirement: Walking-working surfaces must be inspected on a regular basis and as necessary, and must be maintained in a safe condition. Any surface with a hazardous condition must be taken out of service and either guarded to prevent employee use or corrected/repaired before employees use it again. When structural repairs are involved, a qualified person must perform or supervise the repair.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the walking-working surface inspection logs for the most recent quarter. Confirm: inspections occur at documented intervals; identified hazards are tracked to corrective action closure; any surface removed from service is physically barricaded (not just flagged on a log). Sample 10% of corrective actions from inspection logs (minimum 3) and trace each to a closed work order or documented repair.

items.requirement: All ladders must meet the following general construction and use requirements: (1) Rungs, steps, and cleats must be parallel, level, and uniformly spaced 10-14 inches apart (center-to-center); (2) Minimum clear width of 11.5 inches for portable ladders, 16 inches for fixed ladders; (3) Ladder surfaces must be free of puncture and laceration hazards; (4) Ladders must be inspected before each shift and defective ladders must be immediately tagged 'Dangerous: Do Not Use' and removed from service; (5) Employees must face the ladder when climbing or descending; (6) At least one hand must grasp the ladder at all times; (7) No objects or loads carried that could cause loss of balance.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of ladders at the facility (minimum 5 ladders — include a mix of portable, fixed, and rolling). For each: (1) confirm the inspection date on the tag is current; (2) check rung spacing with a tape measure on 1-2 rungs; (3) check the minimum clear width; (4) look for cracked or broken rungs, bent side rails, missing feet or shoes, corrosion on metal ladders, and cracks or checks in wooden ladders; (5) observe whether ladders in storage are labeled correctly; (6) ask employees to demonstrate the correct climbing technique.

items.requirement: Portable ladders must additionally meet these requirements: (1) Not loaded beyond maximum intended load; (2) Used only on stable and level surfaces, or secured to prevent displacement; (3) Secured or guarded with barriers when placed in doorways, passageways, or driveways; (4) Not moved or extended while an employee is on the ladder; (5) No single-rail portable ladders in use; (6) When used for access to an upper landing surface, side rails must extend at least 3 feet above the upper landing; (7) Not placed on boxes, barrels, or other unstable bases to gain height; (8) Not tied or fastened together to provide added length unless designed for such use.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe portable ladder use at 10% of locations where they are deployed (minimum 3 locations). Confirm: the 3-foot extension above upper landing surfaces where access is being made; no ladders placed on unstable bases; no single-rail ladders in use anywhere. Ask employees to demonstrate how they secure a ladder placed in a doorway or near a path of travel.

items.requirement: Fixed ladders must meet: (1) Minimum perpendicular clearance of 7 inches from centerline of rungs to nearest permanent object behind the ladder; (2) Side rails extend at least 42 inches above the top access level; (3) For through-ladders, extensions omit the rungs and flare the side rails 24-30 inches apart; (4) Pitch not greater than 90 degrees from horizontal; (5) Clear width of at least 15 inches on each side of the centerline; (6) Fixed ladders extending more than 24 feet above a lower level must have a ladder safety system (preferred) or cage/well.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of fixed ladders at the facility (minimum 5 fixed ladders). For ladders over 24 feet: confirm a ladder safety system (self-retracting lifeline system) or cage/well is installed. For all fixed ladders: confirm 42-inch side rail extension above the landing; check clearance behind rungs by estimating or measuring the space behind the rung centerline. Look for clogged cages or deteriorated ladder safety system components.

items.requirement: Mobile ladder stands and platforms must meet: (1) Step width of at least 16 inches; (2) Slip-resistant step and platform surfaces; (3) Load capacity of at least 4 times the maximum intended load; (4) Wheels/casters capable of supporting proportional share of 4 times the maximum intended load; (5) Handrails with vertical height 29.5-37 inches on units with top step height of 4 feet or more; (6) Maximum work-surface height must not exceed 4 times the shortest base dimension (unless stabilized); (7) A system to prevent horizontal movement when an employee is on the unit.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of mobile ladder stands and platforms in use (minimum 3 units). Confirm: the wheel lock or brake system functions; the top step height does not exceed 4 times the shortest base dimension (tip-over hazard); handrails are present on units with top steps at or above 4 feet; load capacity marking is visible. Ask employees to demonstrate locking the wheels before climbing.

items.requirement: Step bolts used for climbing structures must meet requirements for dimensions (minimum 4.5-inch tread depth, minimum 8-inch spacing, minimum 4.5-inch width), slip resistance, and load capacity (minimum 250 lbs). Manhole steps must be corrosion-resistant (plastic-encapsulated steel or non-corrosive material) and capable of supporting 250 lbs minimum.
items.evidence_protocol: If the facility has utility vaults, manholes, or structures with step bolts (common in outdoor utility or process areas): inspect 10% of step bolt installations and manhole step locations (minimum 2 locations). Confirm: no steps are missing, corroded through, or loose; spacing is sufficient to allow safe use; and the steps are labeled or included in the facility's walking-working surface inspection program.

items.requirement: Standard stairways must meet: (1) Width of at least 22 inches; (2) Rise height (riser) of 6.5 to 9.5 inches (uniform throughout the flight, with maximum variation of 3/8 inch); (3) Tread depth of at least 9.5 inches (uniform throughout the flight, with maximum variation of 3/8 inch); (4) Non-slip surfaces on stair treads; (5) Stairway angle between 30 and 50 degrees from horizontal.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of stairways at the facility (minimum 5 stairways). For each: (1) observe whether tread surfaces are in good condition and non-slip (rubber nosing or abrasive strips present and not worn through); (2) check for any broken, cracked, or missing treads; (3) check riser height uniformity (a single uneven step is a significant trip hazard — measure the first and last riser height if uniformity appears compromised); (4) confirm adequate width for the level of use.

items.requirement: Spiral, ship, and alternating-tread stairs may only be used where it is not practical to install a standard stairway. Each type has specific design requirements. Spiral stairs: minimum 26-inch inside diameter, 7.5-inch minimum tread depth. Ship stairs: 50-70 degree angle, 8-inch minimum tread depth. Alternating-tread stairs: 50-70 degree angle, 8.5-inch minimum tread depth.
items.evidence_protocol: If any non-standard stair types are present: confirm they are used only in locations where a standard stair is not practical (document the reason for selection). Confirm each type meets its specific design requirements. Confirm handrails are installed on alternating-tread stairs and ship stairs.

items.requirement: Dockboards (bridge plates) must be: (1) Strong enough to carry the load imposed upon them; (2) Secured in position (to the dock or vehicle) by anchors or equivalent means before use to prevent accidental movement; (3) Marked with the rated load capacity; (4) Not loaded beyond their rated load capacity.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of dockboards in use at the facility (minimum 3 dockboards). Confirm: load capacity markings are visible; the securing mechanism (anchor hooks, lip hooks, or chains) is present and used; ask a dock employee to demonstrate how they secure the dockboard before allowing forklift traffic across it. Look for bent, cracked, or corroded dockboards that may be structurally compromised.

items.requirement: Scaffolds used at this facility must meet the same requirements as those found in OSHA's Construction standards at 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L, including: (1) Load rating and capacity markings; (2) Planking or decking of proper grade and condition; (3) Guardrails, mid-rails, and toe boards on all open sides and ends; (4) Erected, moved, dismantled, or altered only under the supervision of a competent person; (5) Inspected by a competent person before each work shift and after any occurrence that could affect structural integrity.
items.evidence_protocol: If scaffolding is used for maintenance or construction work at this facility: walk 10% of erected scaffolds (minimum 2 structures). Confirm: a tagged inspection has been completed for the current work shift; guardrails are present on all open sides; toe boards are in place to prevent tools from falling; the scaffold tag or log identifies the competent person who performed the inspection. If scaffolding appears structurally unstable or shows damaged components, document immediately.

items.requirement: Rope descent systems must have anchorages certified by a qualified person to support at least 5,000 lbs per worker. Before each use, the rope, descent controller, carabiner, harness, and anchor must be inspected. Ropes must not be used if defective.
items.evidence_protocol: If rope descent systems are used for exterior maintenance or window washing activities: confirm the anchorage certification documentation (qualified engineer certification) is on file and current. Confirm users are trained and that a pre-use inspection checklist is completed before each descent.

items.requirement: The employer must ensure that each employee exposed to a fall hazard of 4 feet or more to a lower level is protected by a guardrail system, safety net system, or personal fall protection system. Additionally, fall protection is required at: (1) unprotected sides and edges; (2) hoist areas (guardrail or gate required); (3) holes in walking surfaces (cover or guardrail); (4) ramps, runways, and walkways; (5) walking surfaces above dangerous equipment; (6) wall and floor openings; (7) fixed ladders over 24 feet; (8) stairways with unprotected sides or edges; (9) scaffolds; (10) low-slope roofs.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of all elevated areas, elevated platforms, mezzanines, open floor holes, and areas adjacent to floor openings (minimum 10 locations). For each location: confirm the appropriate fall protection system is in place; measure or estimate the fall height; check guardrail integrity (shake each rail — it must withstand 200 lbs of outward force); confirm all floor/wall openings have either guardrails or covers. Document every unguarded edge, missing guardrail section, or uncovered opening found.

items.requirement: Employees must be protected from falling objects through toeboards, screens, guardrail systems, canopies, or barricades whenever work is performed above other employees or in areas where overhead work creates a dropped-object hazard.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of areas where elevated work occurs (maintenance catwalks, mezzanine edges, overhead crane work) to 10% of locations with overhead work (minimum 5 locations). Confirm: toeboards (minimum 3.5 inches in height) are present on all elevated platforms with guardrails where objects could fall; or a barricade prevents employees from working below; or debris netting is installed. Dropped-object incidents are among the most preventable serious injuries — document any overhead work area without protection.

items.subsection: Fall Protection Systems — Criteria and Practices
items.requirement: Guardrail systems must: (1) Be between 39 and 45 inches in height (measured from the walking-working surface to the top of the top rail); (2) Withstand a force of 200 lbs applied in any outward or downward direction within 2 inches of the top edge; (3) Include a mid-rail (at approximately mid-height between the top rail and walking surface) or equivalent; (4) Have no openings large enough for an employee to fall through; (5) If made of wire rope, have rope capable of withstanding 200 lbs; (6) Not have any horizontal members acting as a ladder between the top rail and the walking surface.
items.evidence_protocol: Evalute guardrail height and guardrailed locations (minimum 10). Height must be 39-45 inches. Physically attempt to push or pull the top rail — it should be rigid. Confirm mid-rails or equivalent are present. Look for damaged or missing mid-rail sections, guardrail posts that are loose, or gaps in the system wide enough for a person to fall through.

items.subsection: Fall Protection Systems — Criteria and Practices
items.requirement: Covers for holes in floors, roofs, and platforms must: (1) Be capable of supporting at least twice the weight of employees, equipment, and materials that may be imposed on the cover at any one time; (2) Be secured to prevent accidental displacement — not just set in place; (3) Be color-coded or marked 'HOLE' or 'COVER' to provide warning.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of hole cover locations (minimum 5 covers). For each: attempt to displace the cover — it must require a deliberate action to remove (cleat, chain, bolt, or other securing mechanism); confirm the cover is marked 'HOLE' or 'COVER' or color-coded in a warning color; check the cover for structural adequacy (no cracks, no excessive deflection under load). Document any unsecured or unmarked hole covers.

items.subsection: Fall Protection Systems — Criteria and Practices
items.requirement: Handrails and stair rail systems must: (1) Handrail top surface height: 30-38 inches above stair nosing; (2) Stair rail system top edge height: 36-42 inches above stair nosing; (3) Capable of withstanding a force of 200 lbs applied in any downward or outward direction at any point along the top edge; (4) Smooth surfaces to prevent lacerations; (5) Continuous the full length of the stair flight (from top riser to bottom riser) with no gaps that could catch a sleeve or fall restraint.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of stairways and measure handrail heights at the top and bottom of each stair (minimum 5 stairways). Compare to the 30-38 inch handrail requirement. Test stability: apply downward and outward force by hand. Check for continuity — no missing sections between top and bottom landings. Look for any handrails with sharp edges, splinters, or protrusions that could cause lacerations.

items.subsection: Fall Protection Systems — Criteria and Practices
items.requirement: Fixed ladders over 24 feet above a lower level must be equipped with a ladder safety system or a personal fall arrest system. Cages and wells are permitted only for ladders installed before November 19, 2018 (and must be replaced with a ladder safety system or PFAS by November 18, 2036). Ladder safety systems and personal fall arrest systems used on ladders must allow the employee to climb and descend without continually having to adjust the system.
items.evidence_protocol: Identify 100% of fixed ladders at the facility that extend more than 24 feet above a lower level. For each: confirm a ladder safety system (self-retracting lifeline or climb assist device) or personal fall arrest system is installed. If a legacy cage or well is still in use (installed before November 19, 2018), note the compliant replacement deadline of November 18, 2036 and confirm the cage meets dimensional requirements in the interim.

items.subsection: Fall Protection Systems — Criteria and Practices
items.requirement: Personal fall arrest systems (PFAS) used to protect against falls from walking-working surfaces must: (1) Limit maximum arresting force to 1,800 lbs; (2) Be rigged to minimize free-fall distance to 6 feet; (3) Bring the employee to a complete stop with a deceleration distance no greater than 3.5 feet; (4) Not allow the employee to contact a lower level or obstruction; (5) Have anchorage capable of supporting 5,000 lbs per attached employee; (6) Be used with a full-body harness (not a body belt for fall arrest).
items.evidence_protocol: For 10% of locations where employees use PFAS for work at heights beyond guardrail protection (minimum 3 locations): confirm the total fall clearance (free fall + deceleration + harness height + safety factor) clears the lower level or obstruction. Confirm anchorage points are rated at 5,000 lbs (anchor certification or structural engineer confirmation). Confirm full-body harnesses — not body belts — are used for fall arrest.

items.requirement: Before being exposed to a fall hazard, each employee must be trained by a qualified person in: (1) The nature of fall hazards in the work area; (2) The correct procedures for installing, inspecting, operating, maintaining, and disassembling the personal fall protection systems to be used; (3) The use and operation of the walking-working surfaces, guardrail systems, and personal fall protection systems; (4) The correct procedures for equipment and materials handling in work area; (5) The maximum intended load-carrying capacities of the equipment to be used. Retraining is required when the employer has reason to believe an employee lacks the required understanding or when equipment or surfaces change.
items.evidence_protocol: Request fall protection training records for 10% of employees who work in areas requiring fall protection (minimum 10 employees). Confirm initial training was completed before first exposure to fall hazards. Interview 10% of employees working at heights (minimum 5). Ask: 'How do you inspect your harness before use?' 'What would you do if you found a fall while using a harness?' 'Who is responsible for anchoring points on this job?' Document gaps between training content and actual equipment/work conditions.

items.requirement: Employer records work-related fatalities, injuries, and illnesses on the OSHA 300 Log.
items.evidence_protocol: Pull the OSHA 300 Log for the current calendar year. Cross-reference against the incident/case management system or first aid log. Sample 10% of all reported injuries and illnesses from the audit period (minimum 3 cases) — confirm each recordable case appears on the 300 Log and that non-recordable cases are appropriately excluded.

items.requirement: Employer uses the correct OSHA forms: OSHA 300 Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses, OSHA 300-A Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses, and OSHA 301 Injury and Illness Incident Report (or an approved equivalent).
items.evidence_protocol: Request the three required forms. Confirm they are the current OSHA versions (or an approved equivalent). If an alternative to the OSHA 301 is used, confirm it is readable, understandable, and completed using the same instructions as the OSHA form it replaces. If computer-based, verify the system can produce equivalent forms on demand.

items.requirement: Each recordable injury/illness is entered on the OSHA 300 Log within 7 calendar days of the employer receiving information that a recordable case has occurred.
items.evidence_protocol: From your case sample (10% of recordable cases, minimum 3), compare the date the employer received information about the injury/illness to the date it was entered on the OSHA 300 Log. The entry must appear within 7 calendar days of that notification date — not 7 days from the injury date.

items.requirement: Computer-based recordkeeping systems are acceptable only if they can produce the equivalent OSHA 300, 300-A, and 301 forms when needed.
items.evidence_protocol: If the site uses electronic recordkeeping (e.g., EHS software), ask the site to generate each form on-screen or print it during the audit. The forms must be producible by the end of the next business day for employee requests and within 4 business hours for government representative requests.

items.requirement: Employee names must not be entered on the OSHA 300 Log for privacy concern cases. Privacy concern cases include: injuries/illnesses affecting an intimate body part or the reproductive system; resulting from sexual assault; mental illness; HIV, hepatitis, or tuberculosis; needlestick or sharps injuries; or any illness voluntarily designated as private by the employee.
items.evidence_protocol: Review 10% of 300 Log entries (minimum 3) that appear to involve privacy-sensitive conditions. Confirm the employee's name is replaced with 'Privacy Case' on the log. Verify no personally identifiable information in the description column could identify the employee.

items.requirement: A separate, confidential list linking each privacy case number to the employee's name must be maintained and stored separately from the OSHA 300 Log.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the confidential privacy case list. Confirm it is stored in a restricted location (not filed with the 300 Log). Verify each privacy case logged on the 300 Log appears on the confidential list. The list should not be accessible to general management.

items.requirement: If the employer has multiple business establishments, a separate OSHA 300 Log must be maintained for each establishment expected to be in operation for one year or longer.
items.evidence_protocol: If this employer operates multiple sites, confirm a site-specific 300 Log exists for this establishment — not a combined log shared across locations. If logs are maintained centrally, confirm the site can produce its own log by end of next business day.

items.requirement: For short-term establishments (operating less than one year), injuries/illnesses must be recorded either on an establishment-specific log, one log covering all short-term establishments, or logs organized by division or region.
items.evidence_protocol: If the employer has any temporary or short-term field locations, confirm which of the three allowable methods is used to capture recordable cases for those locations. Verify that no cases fall through the cracks between the host and field establishment.

items.requirement: Employees who work at multiple locations or at no fixed location must be linked to one business establishment for recordkeeping. Injuries at another employer's establishment are recorded on that establishment's log; injuries elsewhere are recorded on the employee's home establishment log.
items.evidence_protocol: If this site has employees who regularly work at other employer locations or in the field, confirm the site has a defined rule for which log captures their recordable cases. Ask for one example and trace where the case was recorded.

items.requirement: The employer must record recordable injuries/illnesses for all employees under day-to-day supervision, regardless of employment type — including labor, executive, hourly, salaried, part-time, seasonal, migrant, and temporary/staffing agency workers the site supervises.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm with HR or the safety manager how many temporary or contract workers are on site under day-to-day supervision. Review the 300 Log to confirm any recordable cases involving those workers appear. Check the staffing agency contract for language on recordkeeping responsibility.

items.requirement: At year-end, the OSHA 300 Log must be reviewed, the 300-A Annual Summary created and certified by a company executive, and the 300-A posted February 1 through April 30 per 29 CFR 1904.32. The 300-A must contain 8 required data elements. See Evidence column checklist.
items.evidence_protocol:
Review the most recent OSHA 300-A Annual Summary. Verify all 8 required elements are present and completed:
□ (1)  Totals in each column from the OSHA 300 Log (enter zeros if no recordable cases — do not leave blank)
□ (2)  Calendar year covered
□ (3)  Company name
□ (4)  Establishment name
□ (5)  Establishment address
□ (6)  Annual average number of employees covered by the 300 Log (frequently left blank — this is a deficiency)
□ (7)  Total hours worked by all employees covered by the 300 Log (frequently left blank — this is a deficiency)
□ (8)  Certification: signature, printed name, title, and date — by a company executive (owner, officer, highest-ranking official at the establishment, or that person's immediate supervisor)
Also confirm: request posting evidence (date-stamped photo, posting log, or supervisor attestation) that the 300-A was displayed in a conspicuous location from February 1 through April 30.

items.requirement: The employer must retain OSHA 300 Logs, 300-A Annual Summaries, 301 Incident Report Forms, and the privacy case list for 5 years following the end of the calendar year covered.
items.evidence_protocol: Request 300 Logs and 300-A summaries for the 5 prior calendar years. Confirm all are present and have not been destroyed. If electronic, confirm the system retains records with appropriate date stamps.

items.requirement: The OSHA 300 Log must be updated during the 5-year retention period to reflect any newly discovered recordable cases or changes in the classification, description, or outcome of previously recorded cases. Changes are made by lining out the original entry and entering the new information.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager whether any cases were reclassified or had outcomes change (e.g., days away extended after the year closed, a first-aid case later requiring medical treatment). If yes, confirm the 300 Log was updated. Also review for any cases that appear to have an incorrect outcome column checked.

items.requirement: If the business changed ownership during the calendar year, the original owner must record and report work-related injuries and illnesses only for the period it owned the establishment and must transfer all records to the new owner.
items.evidence_protocol: If an ownership change occurred during the audit period, confirm a record-transfer document exists and that the new owner has received and retained prior records. If no ownership change, mark Not Applicable.

items.requirement: Employees and their representatives have the right to access injury and illness records. The employer must: (1) Inform employees how to report an injury/illness promptly and accurately. (2) Inform employees they have the right to report without retaliation. (3) Not discharge or discriminate against employees for reporting. (4) Provide a copy of the 300 Log by end of next business day when requested. (5) Provide a copy of the 301 for their own case by end of next business day. (6) Not charge a fee for the first copy.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager how employees are informed of their right to report injuries and access records. Confirm this information is communicated during onboarding. Interview 1-2 employees: ask if they know how to report an injury and whether they have ever felt discouraged from reporting. Confirm no fee is charged for record copies.

items.requirement: The employer must report to OSHA (or the State Plan equivalent) within 8 hours: any work-related employee fatality. Within 24 hours: any work-related in-patient hospitalization of one or more employees; any work-related amputation; any work-related loss of an eye. Reports may be made by phone to the nearest OSHA Area Office, by phone to 1-800-321-OSHA, or online at osha.gov.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager whether any fatality, hospitalization, amputation, or eye loss has occurred in the past 3 years. If yes, request the OSHA report confirmation (confirmation number, date/time of report) and compare to the incident date. Confirm the site knows all three reporting methods.

items.requirement: The employer must provide copies of the OSHA 300 Log, 300-A, and 301 forms to any authorized government representative (OSHA, NIOSH, or State Plan representative) conducting an inspection or investigation within 4 business hours of the request.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager: 'If an OSHA inspector arrived today and requested your 300 Log, who would pull the records and how long would it take?' Confirm a named individual is responsible and that records are accessible within 4 business hours. Verify this process is documented or trained.

items.requirement: Covered employers must submit their OSHA 300-A data electronically through OSHA's Injury Tracking Application (ITA) by March 2 of the year following the calendar year covered. Coverage depends on establishment size and industry: establishments with 250+ employees must submit 300-A annually; establishments with 20-249 employees in high-hazard industries must also submit; all establishments must submit if notified individually by OSHA.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether this establishment meets the electronic submission threshold (250+ employees, or 20-249 in a covered high-hazard industry per Appendix A to 1904 Subpart E).
