If yes, request the ITA submission confirmation email or screenshot for the most recent year submitted. Confirm submission was completed by the March 2 deadline.

items.requirement: If the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) notifies the employer that it has been selected to participate in the annual Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses, the employer must complete and return the survey form by the deadline specified on the form.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether the site has received a BLS survey notification in the past two years. If yes, request evidence of timely response (completed form or submission confirmation). Note: even employers otherwise exempt from recordkeeping must respond to BLS surveys.

items.requirement: A recordable case is one that is work-related, is a new case, AND meets one or more general recording criteria: (1) death; (2) days away from work; (3) restricted work or job transfer; (4) medical treatment beyond first aid; (5) loss of consciousness; (6) significant injury or illness diagnosed by a physician or PLHCP (including cancer, chronic irreversible disease, fractured or cracked bone, or punctured eardrum).
items.evidence_protocol: Sample 10% of all cases from the audit period (minimum 5 cases, including both recorded and excluded cases). For each case: (1) independently determine work-relatedness; (2) determine if it is a new case; (3) identify whether any general recording criterion applies. Compare your determination to the site's classification on the 300 Log. Document any cases where your determination differs from the site's.

items.requirement: All injuries and illnesses that arise in the work environment are presumed work-related. Exceptions (not recordable): employee was present as a member of the general public; signs/symptoms result solely from a non-work event; voluntary participation in a wellness program; eating/drinking personal food; personal tasks outside assigned work hours; personal grooming or self-medication; motor vehicle accident on a company parking lot while commuting; the common cold or flu; mental illness (unless a licensed professional states it is work-related).
items.evidence_protocol: From your case sample (10%, minimum 5), review any cases the site excluded as non-recordable. Confirm the exclusion falls within one of the eight defined exceptions. Be alert for common misapplications — particularly personal grooming exclusions applied broadly, or 'commuting' exclusions applied to employees who were performing work tasks during travel.

items.requirement: A new case must be recorded if: (1) the employee has not previously experienced a recorded injury/illness of the same type affecting the same part of the body, OR (2) the employee previously had such a case but had completely recovered and a new work event caused symptoms to reappear.
items.evidence_protocol: From your case sample, identify any cases involving a body part or condition the employee had a prior recorded case for. Confirm the site applied the new-case test — fully recovered + new workplace event = new case. Chronic conditions (occupational cancer, asbestosis, silicosis) should be recorded once, not re-recorded each time symptoms recur.

items.requirement: Needlestick and sharps injuries involving another person's blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM) must be recorded on the OSHA 300 Log as an injury. The employee's name must be withheld (privacy case). If the employee is later diagnosed with a bloodborne disease as a result, the record must be updated from injury to illness.
items.evidence_protocol: If the site has any exposure to bloodborne pathogens (maintenance on medical waste equipment, first aid providers, health services): ask whether any sharps exposure incidents have occurred in the audit period. If yes, confirm they appear on the 300 Log as privacy cases and that any resulting diagnoses were used to update the record.

items.requirement: If an employee is removed from a job due to an OSHA medical removal standard (e.g., lead standard 1910.1025, cadmium standard 1910.1027, hexavalent chromium 1910.1026, noise-induced hearing loss under 1910.95), that removal must be recorded as days away from work or restricted work activity, as applicable. Chemical exposure removals must be identified as poisoning on the 300 Log.
items.evidence_protocol: Identify whether any OSHA standards with medical removal provisions apply at this facility (confirm with the safety manager). Sample 10% of MRP-triggered removals from the audit period (minimum 1) and verify they are recorded correctly on the 300 Log.

items.requirement: Work-related hearing loss is recordable when: (1) the employee has experienced a Standard Threshold Shift (STS) — an average change in hearing threshold of 10 dB or more at 2000, 3000, and 4000 Hz in one or both ears — AND (2) the employee's total hearing level is 25 dB or more above audiometric zero (averaged at 2000, 3000, and 4000 Hz) in the same ear(s). Age adjustment is permitted for the STS but not the 25 dB level.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the audiometric testing program results for the audit period. Confirm the audiologist or program administrator has flagged any STS cases and applied both the STS test and the 25 dB level test. Sample 10% of STS cases flagged in the past year (minimum 2) and verify the recordability determination and 300 Log entry. Confirm STS cases are identified in the hearing loss column of the 300 Log.

items.requirement: A work-related tuberculosis (TB) infection is recordable when: (1) the employee was occupationally exposed to a known case of active TB, AND (2) the employee subsequently develops a TB infection as evidenced by a positive skin test or diagnosis by a PLHCP.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether this facility has any potential TB exposure (healthcare adjacency, corrections, homeless shelter proximity). If yes, confirm a TB surveillance protocol is in place and that any positive skin test conversions following known occupational exposure are evaluated for recordability. If TB exposure is not present, mark Not Applicable.

items.requirement: Employers with 10 or fewer employees at all times during the entire calendar year are partially exempt from routine OSHA recordkeeping. However, even exempt employers must: (1) report fatalities, hospitalizations, amputations, and eye losses to OSHA; and (2) maintain records and respond to BLS surveys if selected.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm site headcount at all times during the calendar year. If the site ever exceeded 10 employees — even temporarily (seasonal peak, temp workers, project-based hiring) — the exemption does not apply for that year. Manufacturing facilities the size of NGC typically do not qualify.

items.requirement: Establishments classified in a low-hazard industry listed in 29 CFR 1904 Subpart B Appendix A are partially exempt from routine OSHA recordkeeping. Low-hazard industries include retail, finance, insurance, real estate, and specific service industries. Gypsum manufacturing is NOT on the exempt list.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm this establishment's primary NAICS code with the site manager. Verify the NAICS code is not on the partially-exempt low-hazard industry list (Appendix A to 29 CFR 1904 Subpart B). Manufacturing operations like gypsum production are not exempt and must maintain full records.

items.requirement: The OSHA 'Job Safety and Health: It's the Law' poster (or the applicable State Plan equivalent) must be posted in a conspicuous place where notices to employees are customarily posted. The poster must not be defaced, destroyed, or covered. Reproductions and facsimiles are acceptable if at least 8.5 x 14 inches with 10-point print.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to the main employee bulletin board, break room, time clock area, and any other locations where notices are posted. Confirm the current federal or State Plan OSHA poster is displayed and legible. Note if it is an outdated version (compare the revision date). Free current copies are available at osha.gov.

items.requirement: If an employer obtains copies of OSHA regulations or applicable standards, those copies must be made available for inspection and copying by any employee or authorized representative at the establishment where the employee is employed, on the same day a request is made.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager how an employee would access the applicable OSHA standards if they requested them today. Confirm a mechanism exists — whether a binder in the safety office, an intranet link, or printed copies — and that it is accessible to all employees without management approval.

items.requirement: Immediately upon receipt of any OSHA citation, the employer must post an unedited copy of the citation at or near each place where the alleged violation occurred. The citation must remain posted until the violation has been abated OR for 3 working days — whichever is longer. The citation must remain posted even while being contested.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether any OSHA citations have been received in the past 3 years. If yes, confirm the site has documentation of posting (a log entry, photo, or supervisor attestation). Confirm the citation was not altered, defaced, or covered. If citations are currently open, confirm they are posted today.

items.requirement: For serious, repeat, and willful violations involving movable equipment, the employer must attach a warning tag (or a copy of the citation) to the operating controls or cited component before the equipment moves within or between worksites. The tag must identify the nature of the violation and the location of the citation.
items.evidence_protocol: If any citations involving movable equipment (forklifts, cranes, mobile equipment) have been received, confirm the tagging process was followed before the equipment was moved. If no movable equipment citations, mark Not Applicable.

items.requirement: Cited violations must be abated by the date set in the citation or as extended by OSHA. Within 10 calendar days after the abatement date, the employer must certify to OSHA in writing that each cited violation has been abated. For willful/repeat violations and serious violations requiring abatement documentation, the employer must also submit supporting evidence (photos, receipts, records).
items.evidence_protocol: Request the abatement certification letters or submissions for any citations received. Confirm they were submitted within 10 calendar days of the abatement date. For any serious citations requiring documentation, confirm supporting evidence was included.

items.requirement: All required abatement submissions to OSHA must include: (1) employer name and address; (2) inspection number; (3) citation and item numbers; (4) a statement that the information is accurate; and (5) the employer's signature and authorized representative's signature.
items.evidence_protocol: Review a sample abatement submission and confirm all five required elements are present. A submission missing any element is deficient and may result in a failure to abate finding.

items.requirement: The employer must inform affected employees and their representatives about all abatement activities by posting a copy (or summary) of each submission near the violation location for at least 3 working days. Employees must also be informed of their right to examine and copy abatement documents.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that employee notification was completed for any cited violations. Ask the safety manager how employees were informed. Check for posting of abatement documents or meeting minutes referencing communication to affected employees.

items.requirement: Some states require employers to establish a written Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP), maintain a safety committee, or meet other requirements that exceed federal OSHA. These requirements are typically found in the State Plan's own standards or in state workers' compensation rules.
items.evidence_protocol: Identify the state in which this establishment is located and confirm whether the state operates under a State Plan (rather than federal OSHA). If yes, identify any state-specific requirements (e.g., California's IIPP under Title 8 CCR 3203, Washington's Accident Prevention Program, Michigan's MIOSHA requirements). Confirm those programs are documented and active.

items.requirement: A written energy control program must be established and consist of three elements: (1) documented energy control procedures (ECPs) for each machine/equipment; (2) employee training specific to each role; and (3) periodic inspections of the ECPs at least annually.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written LOTO/energy control program document. Confirm it explicitly addresses all three required elements. Check the revision date — the program should reflect current equipment. Confirm it is readily accessible to authorized employees (not locked in a manager's office).

items.requirement: Energy Control Procedures (ECPs) must be developed, documented, and used for each machine/equipment with hazardous energy. Each ECP must address 5 required content areas per 29 CFR 1910.147(c)(4). See Evidence column checklist.
items.evidence_protocol:
Request a complete list of all machines/equipment requiring ECPs. Sample 10% (minimum 5). For each sampled ECP, verify all 5 required elements are present and evaluate against the actual machine on the floor:
□ (1)  Steps to shut down and de-energize the machine using the established stopping procedure
□ (2)  Steps for placement, removal, and transfer of lockout/tagout devices
□ (3)  Requirements for testing or positioning to verify isolation (the try-out test)
□ (4)  Type and magnitude of each energy source (electrical voltage, hydraulic PSI, pneumatic PSI, stored mechanical energy, thermal, gravity)
□ (5)  Methods for controlling each energy type (which valve to close, which disconnect to lock, which bleeder to open, how to block gravity-loaded parts)
Walk each sampled ECP to the actual machine: confirm every isolation point listed on the procedure exists on the equipment and is accessible. Flag any generic or copy-paste ECPs shared across multiple different machine types — these require correction.

items.requirement: When a tagout device is used on an energy-isolating device that cannot be locked out, the tagout device must be attached at the same location where a lockout device would have been attached, if it were possible to do so.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of the facility's energy-isolating devices (minimum 3 locations) that are identified as tagout-only. Confirm tags are attached directly to the energy-isolating device itself — not to adjacent piping, nearby conduit, or the machine body. Confirm the ECP documents why lockout is infeasible for each tagout-only application.

items.requirement: Where tagout is used in lieu of lockout, the employer must demonstrate that the tagout program will provide a level of safety equivalent to lockout. Equivalency is achieved by implementing additional safety measures such as: removing an isolating circuit element; blocking a controlling switch; opening an extra disconnecting device; or removing a valve handle.
items.evidence_protocol: For each tagout-only application (from LO-3 sample): confirm the ECP documents at least one additional safety measure beyond just affixing a tag. Ask the authorized employee performing tagout to explain and demonstrate the additional measure. Tagout alone, without these supplemental measures, does not meet the equivalency standard.

items.requirement: The employer must provide protective materials and hardware — including locks, tags, chains, wedges, key blocks, adapter pins, self-locking fasteners, and other hardware — as necessary to isolate, secure, or block machines or equipment from energy. This equipment must be provided at no cost to the employee.
items.evidence_protocol: Physically inspect LOTO hardware stations (there should be at least one per work area). Confirm the station is stocked with sufficient locks, tags, hasps, and hardware for the number of authorized employees on the largest shift. Ask authorized employees whether they have ever been unable to obtain a LOTO device when needed. Confirm no employee purchases their own LOTO hardware.

items.requirement: LOTO devices must meet four criteria: (1) Durable — capable of withstanding the environment and the use for which they are intended, including exposure to corrosive chemicals; tags must be able to withstand a 50-lb pullout force. (2) Standardized — same color, shape, or size within the facility, or at least the same print and format. (3) Substantial — locks must be substantial so they cannot be removed except by excessive force. (4) Identifiable — individually identified (unique to each authorized employee) and used exclusively for LOTO purposes — not for any other use.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect LOTO devices at 10% of LOTO hardware stations (minimum 2 stations). For each: confirm locks are individually keyed and assigned to one authorized employee; confirm tags bear a warning legend ('Do Not Start/Open/Energize') and the authorized employee's name; confirm the same color/style is used consistently throughout the facility; physically test a tag's attachment — it should require at least 50 lbs of force to remove. Ask whether any LOTO devices are used for non-energy-control purposes (e.g., tool storage locks).

items.requirement: At least annually, an authorized employee (other than the one using the procedure) must inspect each ECP in use and certify that the inspection was conducted. For lockout procedures, the inspection must include a review with each authorized employee of their responsibilities under the procedure. For tagout procedures, the inspection must include a review of tagout limitations in addition to the procedure review. The certification must document the machine/equipment, the date of inspection, the employees involved, and the inspector's name.
items.evidence_protocol: Request annual ECP inspection records for 100% of the current calendar year (or the most recent 12 months). Confirm: (1) every ECP in active use has been inspected within the past 12 months; (2) the inspector is an authorized employee — not the person who normally uses that procedure; (3) the certification document identifies the machine, date, employees involved, and inspector. Note any ECPs with no inspection on file — this is among the most frequently cited LOTO deficiencies.

items.requirement: Training must be provided to ensure all employees understand the purpose and function of the energy control program and have the knowledge and skills required for their role. Authorized employees must be trained on: (1) recognition of applicable hazardous energy sources; (2) type and magnitude of energy in the workplace; and (3) methods and means necessary for energy isolation and control. Affected employees must be trained on: (1) the purpose and use of the energy control procedure; and (2) the prohibition on restarting or re-energizing equipment under LOTO. Other employees who work in areas where LOTO is used must be trained on the prohibition and the purpose of LOTO.
items.evidence_protocol: Request LOTO training records for 100% of authorized employees and a 10% sample of affected employees (minimum 3). Confirm: (1) initial training was completed before the employee performed LOTO; (2) training records identify which ECP(s) the employee is authorized on; (3) training covers the specific energy types at this facility. Interview 1-2 authorized employees: ask them to explain the type and magnitude of energy on one of their assigned machines, the order of lockout steps, and what they do at shift change if their lock must stay applied.

items.requirement: Retraining must be provided when: (1) there is reason to believe the employee does not have the required knowledge or skills (e.g., following an incident or observed deviation); (2) there is a change in job assignments; (3) there is a change in machines, equipment, or processes that presents a new hazardous energy hazard; or (4) there is a change in the energy control procedures.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask for a list of equipment additions or process changes in the past 12 months. Confirm retraining records exist for affected authorized employees when new equipment was introduced. Review any near-miss or LOTO incident reports — confirm retraining was completed as a corrective action. Sample 10% of new equipment commissioning records (minimum 1) to trace the ECP development and employee retraining.

items.requirement: LOTO procedures must be performed only by the authorized employees who are performing the servicing or maintenance. Affected employees (operators) must not apply or remove lockout/tagout devices.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of production operators (minimum 3). Ask: 'If you needed to clear a jam on your machine, who would apply the lockout?' The correct answer is a trained and authorized employee — not the operator themselves, unless the operator has also been designated as an authorized employee for that specific task. Review any documented cases where an operator was observed applying or removing a lock.

items.requirement: Affected employees must be notified by the employer or the authorized employee both: (1) before a lockout or tagout device is applied; and (2) after the device is removed and before the machine is re-started.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of affected employees (minimum 3). Ask: 'How are you told when a lock is being put on your machine? And when it's been removed?' Confirm the ECP or written program describes a specific notification method (departmental notification, physical communication, visual signal). Review any LOTO-related incident reports for cases where communication failed.

items.requirement: When lockout/tagout devices must be temporarily removed from an energy-isolating device to test or position the machine, the following specific sequence must be followed: (1) Clear the machine of tools and materials; (2) remove employees from the machine area; (3) remove lockout/tagout devices; (4) energize and proceed with testing/positioning; (5) de-energize all systems; (6) reapply energy control measures before continuing servicing work.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the maintenance supervisor whether testing or repositioning during maintenance ever requires temporary re-energization (e.g., bump tests, valve positioning checks, alignment verification). If yes, request the ECP section covering this sequence and confirm all six steps are documented. Observe or interview an authorized employee to confirm they follow this specific order.

items.requirement: When contractors or outside personnel engage in LOTO activities at this facility, the on-site employer and the outside employer must inform each other of their respective lockout/tagout procedures. The on-site employer must ensure that their own employees follow the outside employer's energy control procedures, or vice versa, so both are protected.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the most recent contract or pre-job safety meeting documentation for a contractor that performed maintenance or servicing at this facility in the past year. Confirm the host shared its confined space LOTO requirements and the contractor shared its own procedures. Verify the two programs are compatible or that a coordination protocol exists. Sample 10% of contractor LOTO jobs from the past year (minimum 1).

items.requirement: When a job requiring LOTO involves more than one authorized employee, each employee must have personal protection: either their own personal lock on the energy-isolating device or their own personal lock on a group lockout device (hasp or group lockout box). Each employee's personal lock must be removed only by that employee.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask how group LOTO jobs are managed. Confirm a group lockout hasp or box is available and used for multi-person jobs. Walk through a multi-person job scenario with an authorized employee: confirm each person places their own lock and no one can re-energize the equipment until every personal lock has been individually removed by its owner.

items.requirement: Procedures must be in place to ensure that LOTO protection is maintained during shift or personnel changes. The minimum protection method is: the incoming authorized employee applies their lock before the outgoing employee removes theirs — so the equipment is never left unprotected during the transition.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager and an authorized employee how shift-change LOTO transfers are handled. Confirm the written procedure requires the incoming employee's lock to be applied before the outgoing employee's lock is removed. Also confirm there is a documented procedure for emergency lock removal when an authorized employee is unavailable (e.g., leaves sick, terminated) — this must require approval by supervision and must not simply involve cutting the lock.

items.subsection: Application and Release of Energy Controls
items.requirement: All energy control applications must follow a documented 7-step sequence per 29 CFR 1910.147(d). ECPs that omit stored/residual energy steps are among the most commonly cited LOTO violations.
items.evidence_protocol:
Select 10% of active ECPs (minimum 3) that involve complex or multiple energy types. Walk each ECP to the actual machine with an authorized employee. Confirm the ECP includes all 7 steps in order:
□ (1)  Notify affected employees that the machine must be shut down and locked/tagged out
□ (2)  Identify the type and magnitude of energy and the hazards posed
□ (3)  Shut down and de-energize the machine using the established stopping procedure
□ (4)  Isolate the machine from each energy source using the energy-isolating device(s)
□ (5)  Apply lockout or tagout device(s) to each energy-isolating device
□ (6)  Release, restrain, or otherwise render safe all stored/residual energy (capacitor bleed-down; spring tension release; hydraulic/pneumatic bleed; blocking gravity-loaded parts; thermal cool-down)
□ (7)  Verify isolation is complete — attempt to start the machine (try-out test) or test with a meter BEFORE touching any part
Grade down significantly for any ECP that addresses only electrical isolation and omits stored/residual energy steps.

items.subsection: Application and Release of Energy Controls
items.requirement: Before energy is restored and LOTO devices are removed, a documented 6-step sequence must be followed per 29 CFR 1910.147(e). An emergency lock removal procedure must also exist for situations where the authorized employee is unavailable.
items.evidence_protocol:
Review the energy release section of 10% of ECPs (minimum 3). Confirm the ECP includes all 6 steps in order:
□ (1)  Ensure work on the machine is complete
□ (2)  Ensure all tools, materials, and non-essential items are removed from the machine
□ (3)  Ensure all employees are safely positioned away from the machine
□ (4)  Notify affected employees that LOTO devices are being removed and energy will be restored
□ (5)  Remove each lockout/tagout device only by the authorized employee who applied it
□ (6)  Restore energy
Also confirm: a written emergency lock removal procedure exists for situations where the authorized employee has left the site. Ask the supervisor: 'What do you do if an employee goes home sick and leaves their lock on equipment?' — the answer must reference a written procedure requiring supervisor authorization (never simply cutting the lock without authorization).

items.requirement: Exit routes must be permanent parts of the workplace. Each exit must be a permanent structural component — not a temporary door, movable partition, or makeshift opening — and must lead directly to a safe area outside the building.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of identified exit routes (minimum 3). Confirm each exit is a permanent structural component. Look for any exits that route through a space that could be locked, occupied, or blocked during an emergency. Note any exit that depends on a door that could be locked from the other side.

items.requirement: Each exit must be separated from other areas of the building by construction materials that have the required fire resistance ratings. Openings into an exit enclosure must be limited — only those necessary for occupant use.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk exit stairwells and corridors. Confirm enclosing walls, ceilings, and floors have appropriate fire-resistance ratings. Look for holes, penetrations, or unsealed conduit openings in exit enclosure walls. Confirm doors into the exit enclosure are self-closing fire-rated doors (not propped open).

items.requirement: At least two exit routes must be available to permit prompt evacuation. Exception: one exit is acceptable if the number of employees, size, occupancy, or arrangement allows all employees to evacuate safely.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the number of exit routes from each major work area. For multi-story buildings or areas with large employee populations, confirm at least two exits are available and that they are separated by sufficient distance so a single event cannot block both.

items.requirement: Each exit discharge must lead directly to the street, walkway, refuge area, public way, or other open space with room for evacuated employees. Exit stairs that continue below the exit discharge level must have barriers to prevent employees from accidentally continuing past the exit discharge level.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to the exterior discharge point of each sampled exit route. Confirm the discharge location is an open, unobstructed space large enough to accommodate building occupants. For multi-story exit stairs, confirm a barrier (door, gate, or signage) at the exit discharge level prevents employees from continuing to a lower level.

items.requirement: Exit doors must be unlocked and able to be freely opened from the inside at all times without a key, tool, special knowledge, or effort. Delayed-egress hardware (up to 15-second delay) is permitted only in specific limited applications and only where approved by the authority having jurisdiction.
items.evidence_protocol: Test 10% of exit doors (minimum 3) during the audit. Push open the door from the inside without any key or special device — it must open freely. Confirm no padlocks, deadbolts, or keyed locks are installed on the interior side of exit doors. If delayed-egress hardware is present, confirm it is specifically approved for the application.

items.requirement: Exit doors must meet design requirements: side-hinged; open in the direction of exit travel for rooms with 50 or more occupants or hazardous areas; not be a sliding, revolving, or overhead door.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of exit doors (minimum 3). Confirm each is a side-hinged door. For any exit door serving a hazardous area or a room with 50+ occupants, confirm the door swings in the direction of travel (outward). Note any sliding or roll-up doors installed on exit routes.

items.requirement: Exit routes must have adequate capacity for all occupants. Exit route height must be at least 7 feet 6 inches (2.3 m) for the entire length. Width must be sufficient for the number of occupants served, but in no case less than 28 inches (71 cm). No locked room, device, or condition may reduce capacity below required levels.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of exit corridors and stairways (minimum 3). Measure or estimate width — confirm no pinch points below 28 inches. Look for stored materials, equipment, or other obstructions that reduce width. Measure or estimate ceiling height — confirm clearance is at least 7 feet 6 inches throughout.

items.requirement: Exit routes must be kept free of explosive or highly flammable furnishings, decorations, or other materials. Safeguards against dangerous conditions must be maintained on exit routes.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk all identified exit routes and observe: no flammable materials stored in corridors; no compressed gas cylinders, paint, solvents, or other hazardous materials blocking or lining the exit path; fire door closers and latches are functional.

items.requirement: Each exit and the path of travel to it must be adequately lit so employees can see along the exit route. Lighting must be sufficient for a person to see with normal vision.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk exit routes during normal work hours. Confirm lighting is operational throughout each route, including any stairwells. Ask whether there is emergency backup lighting that activates on power failure — if so, confirm it is tested per the manufacturer's schedule.

items.requirement: Each exit must be clearly marked by a visible sign with the word 'EXIT' in plainly legible letters not less than 6 inches high and 3/4 inch wide stroke. Any door, passageway, or stairway that could be mistaken for an exit must be marked 'NOT AN EXIT' or similar. Each exit route must be marked with signs posted along the route so employees always know the direction of exit travel.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk all exit routes. Confirm: EXIT signs are posted at each exit door and at decision points along the route; signs are illuminated or self-luminescent; no burned-out exit signs; doors that could be mistaken for exits (supply rooms, storage) are clearly marked 'NOT AN EXIT.' Sample 10% of exit signs (minimum 5) and test illumination by covering the power source indicator.

items.requirement: Exit routes must be maintained during construction, repairs, or alterations. If an exit route is temporarily unavailable, an alternate exit must be available. The fire retardant properties of paint or coatings used in exit enclosures must be maintained.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether any construction or renovation is currently ongoing. If yes, confirm alternate exit routes are in place and marked. Ask about any recent painting in exit stairwells — confirm fire-retardant paint was used where required.

items.requirement: An employee alarm system must be operable, audible throughout the building, and maintained. The alarm must provide enough warning time for employees to escape safely before conditions become hazardous.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask for the most recent alarm system test record. Confirm the alarm was audible in all occupied areas (including high-noise production areas and restrooms). Confirm the alarm system is maintained per the manufacturer's schedule and NFPA 72 requirements. Walk to the farthest point in the building from an alarm device and assess audibility.

items.requirement: The employer must have a written Emergency Action Plan (EAP) whenever an OSHA standard requires one or whenever the employer must have an employee alarm system. The EAP must be kept in the workplace and available for employee review.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written EAP. Confirm it is current (review date within the past 12 months or after any significant change to building layout, occupancy, or hazards). Confirm it is physically accessible to all employees — not solely in an electronic system that may be unavailable during an emergency.

items.requirement:
The EAP must contain all of the following required elements:
(1) Procedures for reporting a fire or other emergency
(2) Procedures for emergency evacuation, including type of evacuation and exit route assignments
(3) Procedures to be followed by employees who remain to operate critical plant operations before they evacuate
(4) Procedures to account for all employees after evacuation
(5) Procedures for employees performing rescue or medical duties
(6) The name or job title of every employee who may be contacted by employees who need more information about the plan or an explanation of their duties under the plan
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — review the EAP against all 6 required elements. Mark each: Verified (present and complete), Partial, or Absent.
□ (1) Fire/emergency reporting procedures (who to call, how to activate alarm)
□ (2) Evacuation procedures with exit route map or assignments by area
□ (3) Procedures for critical operation employees who must stay until safe shutdown
□ (4) Employee accounting procedures after evacuation (headcount, assembly point, accountability method)
□ (5) Rescue and medical duty procedures and designated personnel
□ (6) Employee contact for questions about the plan (name and/or job title)
Pay particular attention to elements (4) and (6) — these are frequently incomplete or generic.

items.requirement: The employer must designate and train employees to assist in safe and orderly evacuation. Training must be provided at initial assignment, whenever the plan changes, and at least annually.
items.evidence_protocol: Request training records for designated floor wardens or evacuation assistants. Confirm initial training before assignment and annual refresher. Interview a designated floor warden: ask what their responsibilities are during evacuation, how they account for employees in their area, and where the assembly point is. Confirm they know what to do if a special-needs employee requires assistance.

items.requirement: The employer must review the EAP with each employee at the following times: when the plan is developed or the employee is assigned initially; when the employee's responsibilities change; and when the plan changes.
items.evidence_protocol: Request records showing EAP review with employees. Confirm the review method (drill, training session, orientation). Interview 10% of employees (minimum 5): ask where the nearest exit is from their workstation, where the assembly point is, and who their floor warden is. Gap between training content and employee knowledge is a finding.

items.requirement: Where an OSHA standard requires a Fire Prevention Plan (FPP), it must be in writing, kept in the workplace, and available for employee review. Exception: the employer may communicate the plan orally to employees if there are 10 or fewer employees.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether a Fire Prevention Plan is required by any OSHA standard applicable to this facility (e.g., flammable liquids, spray finishing). If required, request the written FPP. Confirm it is current and accessible.

items.requirement: The Fire Prevention Plan must contain: (1) a list of all major fire hazards, proper handling and storage procedures, potential ignition sources, and the type of fire protection equipment needed; (2) procedures to control accumulations of flammable and combustible waste materials; (3) procedures for regular maintenance of equipment designed to prevent or control sources of ignition or fires; (4) the name or job title of employees responsible for maintaining fire prevention equipment; (5) the name or job title of employees responsible for the control of fuel source hazards.
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — review the FPP against all 5 required elements:
□ (1) Major fire hazards list with handling/storage procedures, ignition sources, and equipment type needed
□ (2) Procedures to control flammable/combustible waste accumulations
□ (3) Maintenance procedures for ignition-control equipment
□ (4) Named responsibility for fire prevention equipment maintenance
□ (5) Named responsibility for fuel source hazard control
For a gypsum manufacturing facility, the FPP should specifically address gypsum dust accumulation (combustible dust), fuel storage, and any natural gas or propane used in the drying process.

items.requirement: The employer must inform employees of the fire hazards of the materials and processes to which they are exposed, and review with each employee those parts of the FPP necessary for self-protection.
items.evidence_protocol: Request evidence that fire hazard information has been communicated to employees (training records, new hire orientation content, toolbox talks). Interview 10% of employees (minimum 5) in areas with fire hazards: ask what fire hazards exist in their work area, what they would do if they discovered a fire, and whether they have been trained to use a fire extinguisher.

items.requirement: Only approved portable fire extinguishers are permitted. Extinguishers must be mounted, located, and identified so they are readily accessible to employees without subjecting them to injury. Extinguishers must not be obstructed or obscured from view.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of fire extinguisher locations (minimum 10). For each: confirm the extinguisher is mounted in its designated location (not sitting on the floor); the mounting location is clearly marked; there are no obstructions blocking access; the unit is visible without needing to move materials. Note any locations where the extinguisher has been removed or the location is unoccupied.

items.requirement: Portable fire extinguishers must be maintained in a fully charged, operable condition and kept in their designated locations. Monthly visual inspections and annual maintenance checks by a licensed service provider are required.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of extinguishers (minimum 10). Confirm: the monthly inspection tag has been initialed within the last 30 days; the pressure gauge needle is in the green zone; the pull pin is intact with the tamper seal; there is no visible physical damage, corrosion, or nozzle clogging; the annual service tag shows service within the past 12 months. Ask to see the extinguisher service records for the past 12 months.

items.requirement: Hydrostatic testing of portable extinguishers is required at intervals depending on extinguisher type: stored-pressure dry chemical = 12 years; CO2 = 5 years; pressurized water = 5 years; Halon = 12 years. Cylinders that fail testing must be condemned.
items.evidence_protocol: Review the hydrostatic test records for 10% of extinguishers (minimum 5). Confirm test dates are within the required intervals for the extinguisher type. Confirm test records are retained. Note any extinguishers whose hydrostatic test date cannot be determined from the shell markings.

items.requirement: Employees who are expected to use portable fire extinguishers must be trained at the time of initial assignment and annually thereafter. The training must cover the general principles of fire extinguisher use and the hazards involved with incipient stage firefighting.
items.evidence_protocol: Request training records for 10% of employees in areas with fire extinguishers where employees are expected to use them (minimum 10 employees). Confirm initial training and annual retraining. Interview 10% of those employees (minimum 3): ask them to identify the type of extinguisher in their area, the classes of fire it covers, and how to operate it (PASS: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep). Note: if the employer's policy is that employees evacuate and do not fight fires, confirm that policy is documented and employees know it.

items.requirement: Automatic sprinkler systems must be designed and installed using good engineering practices. Design and installation must be in accordance with NFPA 13 (Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems) or another nationally recognized standard.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether automatic sprinkler systems are installed. If yes, request the most recent inspection/test report from a licensed sprinkler contractor. Confirm the system was designed and installed in accordance with NFPA 13. Ask for the original acceptance test documentation if available.

items.requirement: Automatic sprinkler systems must be properly maintained and undergo regular acceptance testing. At a minimum: quarterly inspections of control valves; annual inspection of the complete system; 5-year internal pipe inspection; trip test of dry and pre-action systems annually.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the sprinkler system inspection and testing records for the past 12 months. Confirm quarterly control valve inspections, annual full system inspection, and that the system has not had any extended impairments without an approved fire watch. Confirm all deficiencies identified during inspections have been corrected.

items.requirement: Automatic sprinkler systems with more than 20 sprinklers must have a local waterflow alarm that sounds when the system activates. The alarm must be maintained in operating condition.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm a waterflow alarm exists and is operational. Request the most recent alarm test record. Confirm the alarm is audible in occupied areas. For systems connected to a monitoring service, confirm the monitoring contract and most recent communication test.

items.requirement: Sprinkler spacing, placement, and clearance must ensure full coverage. A minimum 18-inch (46 cm) clearance must be maintained between the top of storage and sprinkler deflectors to ensure proper spray pattern distribution.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of storage areas, racking systems, and production areas with sprinkler coverage (minimum 5 areas). For each: measure or estimate clearance between the top of the highest stored material and the sprinkler deflector. Confirm it meets the 18-inch minimum. Note any racking reconfigurations or storage height increases that may have been made since the sprinkler system was designed.

items.requirement: Fire detection systems and all components must be approved. Systems must be restored to normal operating condition as quickly as possible after each test or alarm.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the fire detection system (smoke detectors, heat detectors, manual pull stations) is a listed/approved system. Request documentation of the most recent system test. Confirm that following any alarm or test, the system was restored to full operation promptly.

items.requirement: Fire detectors must be maintained, tested, and adjusted regularly to ensure proper operation. Test schedules must follow the manufacturer's recommendations and NFPA 72 requirements. Detectors that fail testing must be repaired or replaced immediately.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the fire detection system inspection and test records for the past 12 months. Confirm testing was conducted by a qualified contractor per NFPA 72: smoke detectors tested annually; heat detectors tested per the schedule; manual pull stations tested at least annually. Confirm all failed detectors were replaced before the system was returned to service.

items.requirement: Fire detection systems must be protected from corrosive atmospheres, mechanical damage, and environmental conditions that could cause false alarms or system failure. The number, locations, and spacing of detectors must be based on engineering principles and the manufacturer's specifications.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of detector locations (minimum 10 devices). For each: confirm the detector is not painted over, covered, or physically damaged; confirm it is located within the manufacturer's coverage area (not too far from the nearest detector, not in a location with airflow that could prevent smoke from reaching it). Check whether gypsum dust accumulation is addressed — dusty environments can desensitize smoke detectors.

items.requirement: Employee alarm systems must be capable of providing sufficient warning to permit all employees to safely evacuate the building before conditions become hazardous. The alarm must be distinctive from all other sounds in the facility.
items.evidence_protocol: Test the alarm system audibility at 10% of occupied locations (minimum 5 locations), including high-noise production areas, restrooms, and break rooms. Confirm the alarm is clearly distinguishable from production noise, PA announcements, and other operational sounds. Note any dead zones. Ask how employees in hearing protection are notified — confirm a visual (strobe) alarm is provided in high-noise areas.

items.requirement: Employee alarm systems must be properly installed and restored to normal operating condition as quickly as possible after each use. The system must be in continuous operation except during repair or maintenance.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the alarm system installation and commissioning documentation. Confirm the system is currently operational. Ask the maintenance manager the most recent date the system was fully operational (any extended outages are a finding). Confirm an impairment procedure exists for planned outages (fire watch, backup notification system).

items.requirement: Employee alarm systems must be maintained and tested properly. All components must be kept in operating condition except when undergoing maintenance. The employer must establish procedures for testing the alarm and must test the system at least quarterly.
items.evidence_protocol: Request quarterly alarm test records for the past 12 months (minimum 4 test records). Confirm tests were conducted and results documented. Ask who is responsible for conducting the quarterly tests and maintaining the records. Confirm failed components were repaired before the system was returned to service.

items.requirement: Manually operated actuation devices for employee alarm systems must be unobstructed, conspicuous, and readily accessible.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of manual pull stations (minimum 5). For each: confirm the pull station is clearly marked; there are no storage items, equipment, or other materials blocking access; the pull station is at the required mounting height (generally 3.5-5 feet from floor to handle). Note any pull stations that have been painted over or that are difficult to reach.

items.requirement: Where standpipe and hose systems are installed, they must be: properly located with hose connections readily accessible; designed and installed to facilitate prompt use; equipped with hoses and nozzles that are tested and maintained; connected to an adequate water supply.
items.evidence_protocol: If standpipe systems are present: request the most recent inspection records. Confirm hose stations are accessible and the cabinets are not locked or blocked. Inspect 10% of hose stations (minimum 3): check that the hose is properly folded or racked, the nozzle is attached, the valve handle is present, and the hose is not deteriorated or cracked.

items.requirement: States and local jurisdictions often adopt fire codes (NFPA 1, IFC, state-specific codes) that are more stringent than federal OSHA requirements. The site must meet applicable state and local fire marshal requirements, including hot work permits, impairment procedures, and occupancy-specific requirements.
items.evidence_protocol: Identify applicable state and local fire codes for this jurisdiction. Confirm the site's last fire marshal inspection and any outstanding violations or abatement items. If a hot work permit program is required, confirm it is in place and actively used. Ask whether any fire protection system impairments have occurred in the past 12 months and confirm a fire watch was conducted.

items.requirement: Machine guards must be provided to protect persons in the machine area from hazards created by point of operation, ingoing nip points, rotating parts, flying chips, and sparks. Guards must be affixed to the machine where possible and secured to prevent displacement during operations.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of production and maintenance equipment (minimum 10 machines). For each machine: (1) identify all hazard points — point of operation, nip points, rotating shafts/couplings, fan blades, belt/pulley assemblies; (2) confirm a guard is in place for each hazard; (3) attempt to displace the guard by hand — it should require a tool to remove; (4) confirm the guard does not create a new hazard (sharp edges, pinch points from the guard itself). Document any machine operating without a required guard.

items.requirement: Points of operation that expose an employee to injury from rotating, reciprocating, transverse, or cutting actions must be guarded. The guarding device must prevent the operator's hands, arms, or other body parts from making contact with the danger zone during the operating cycle.
items.evidence_protocol: For each production machine in the sample (10%, minimum 10): specifically identify the point of operation — the area where work is actually performed on the material. Confirm the point-of-operation guard prevents an operator from reaching into the danger zone during the operating cycle. Test by attempting to insert your hand toward the danger zone with the guard in place (machine off/locked out) — a proper guard prevents access.

items.requirement: Special hand tools used to place or remove material from a point of operation must be designed to handle material safely, and must not be used in place of proper guarding. The tools must allow the safe placement and removal of materials without the operator placing their hands into the point of operation.
items.evidence_protocol: If special hand tools (push sticks, holding fixtures, feeding devices) are used at any machines in the sample: confirm the tools are dedicated to the machine and appropriate for the task. Confirm no operator is using makeshift tools (scrap lumber, pliers, tape) in place of proper tooling. Confirm the use of hand tools does not replace a required guard — guards and tools are not mutually exclusive.

items.requirement: Revolving drums, barrels, and containers that are used as part of a production process must be guarded by an enclosure that interlocks with the drive mechanism so the drive is disengaged when the guard is opened.
items.evidence_protocol: If the facility has revolving drums, barrels, or containers (common in mixing, blending, or coating operations): confirm each has an enclosing guard and confirm the interlock prevents operation when the guard is open. Test the interlock with the machine off and locked out — confirm the interlock mechanism is functional and not bypassed.

items.requirement: Fan blades must be guarded by a guard with openings no larger than 1/2 inch (1.3 cm) where the periphery of the fan is less than 7 feet from the floor or working level.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of fans and ventilation units where the fan periphery is accessible below 7 feet (minimum 5 units). Confirm a guard is in place and that the guard openings do not exceed 1/2 inch. Fans mounted above 7 feet do not require guarding — confirm the measurement if there is any question.

items.requirement: Machines designed for a fixed location must be securely anchored to prevent movement that could cause a hazard to the operator or others. Unsecured machines that could tip or shift during operation must be anchored or braced.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of fixed production and maintenance machines (minimum 5 machines). Confirm each is anchored to the floor or permanently mounted. Shake or push each machine by hand to assess stability. Pay particular attention to grinders, drill presses, and other equipment with significant vibration — these are prone to walking if not anchored.

items.requirement: Abrasive wheel safety guards must be used on all bench and pedestal grinders. Guards must be designed and sized to retain fragments in case of wheel breakage and must meet ANSI B7.1 design specifications.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of bench and pedestal grinders at the facility. For each: confirm a wheel guard is installed; the guard covers the wheel to the required exposure angles (no more than 90 degrees for bench grinders positioned above center, no more than 180 degrees for grinders positioned on the horizontal centerline); the guard is firmly attached and cannot be easily displaced.

items.requirement: A work rest must be used to support the work on bench and pedestal grinders. The work rest must be: rigidly mounted; adjustable; kept adjusted to within 1/8 inch (3.2 mm) of the wheel; and positioned so the work piece cannot be drawn between the rest and the wheel.
items.evidence_protocol: For each bench and pedestal grinder: confirm the work rest is present, rigid, and adjustable. Measure the gap between the work rest and the wheel — it must not exceed 1/8 inch (3.2 mm). Demonstrate or ask an employee to demonstrate how the work rest is readjusted as the wheel wears down.

items.requirement: All abrasive wheels must be closely inspected and ring-tested ('sounded') immediately before mounting to detect cracks. A cracked wheel has a dull thud rather than a clear metallic ring when tapped. Cracked wheels must not be used.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the person responsible for changing grinding wheels to demonstrate the ring test procedure. Confirm: the wheel is supported through the hole (not laying flat); it is tapped 1-2 inches from the edge at two points 45 degrees apart on each side; a cracked wheel emits a dull thud, not a clear ring. Ask when they last found and discarded a cracked wheel — a 'never' answer warrants a closer look at the inspection program.

items.requirement: Grinding wheels must fit freely on the spindle and remain free under all grinding conditions. The spindle speed of the machine must not exceed the maximum operating speed (RPM) marked on the wheel. Flanges must be the correct size for the wheel and must be maintained in good condition.
items.evidence_protocol: For 10% of grinding wheels in use (minimum 3): confirm the maximum RPM marked on the wheel is equal to or greater than the maximum spindle speed of the machine it is mounted on. Confirm the flanges are the correct type and are not damaged. Confirm blotter pads (compressible washers) are installed between the flanges and the wheel where required.

items.requirement: The facility must provide and ensure that point-of-operation guards or devices protect operators of mechanical power presses from injury. The guard or device must prevent the operator from placing hands in the point-of-operation danger zone during the press operating cycle.
items.evidence_protocol: If mechanical power presses are used: inspect 100% of power presses. For each: confirm a point-of-operation guard or safety device is present and operational. Acceptable devices include: barrier guards, two-hand controls, presence-sensing devices, pull-out devices, hold-out devices, or gates. Confirm the device is adjusted to the specific tooling in use — a guard adjusted for one die size may be inadequate when the tooling is changed.

items.requirement: Mechanical power presses must have adequate machine safeguards on all controls. Foot pedals must be guarded to prevent accidental operation. Two-hand trip mechanisms and two-hand controls must require concurrent activation and must prevent one-hand bypassing.
items.evidence_protocol: For each power press: test foot pedal guards by attempting to trip the press with your foot from outside the normal operating position — the guard should prevent inadvertent activation. For two-hand controls: verify both buttons must be activated simultaneously (within 0.5 seconds of each other); attempt to activate with one hand and then use that elbow or body to hold one button while reaching into the die area — the control should not permit this.

items.requirement: All electrical equipment must be approved (listed, labeled, or certified by a nationally recognized testing laboratory — NRTL — such as UL, CSA, or FM). Equipment must be installed and used in accordance with any instructions included in the listing or labeling.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of electrical panels, motor control centers, and electrical equipment areas (minimum 5 locations). For each piece of equipment: confirm NRTL listing markings (UL, CSA, FM, or other recognized labels) are present on the equipment nameplate. Note any field-fabricated electrical equipment (homemade control boxes, modified panels) that lacks a listing — these require engineering review.

items.requirement: Adequate working space must be maintained around all electrical equipment: minimum 3 feet deep for equipment up to 600V; additional space for higher voltages. Working space must not be used for storage. Equipment must be accessible for maintenance and operation without removing obstructions.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of electrical panels, switchboards, and motor control centers (minimum 10 panels). For each: confirm the required 3-foot working space in front of the panel is clear (not used as storage); the area is wide enough to allow the panel door to open 90 degrees; no permanent or temporary storage is present in the working space. Measure the depth from the panel door to any obstruction — must be 3 feet minimum for ≤600V.

items.requirement: Live parts operating at 50 volts or more must be guarded against accidental contact. Guarding methods include: location in a room or vault accessible only to qualified persons; permanent substantial partitions or screens; location on a suitable balcony or gallery; elevation of 8 feet or more above the floor.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of electrical equipment areas (minimum 5). For each: confirm all live parts (bus bars, terminals, wiring connections) are enclosed or guarded. Look for open knockouts in panels, missing covers on junction boxes, or disconnected conduit leaving conductors exposed. Test 100% of panel covers — confirm all are latched and in place.

items.requirement: Breaker panels and electrical enclosures must be labeled to indicate the purpose or equipment controlled by each circuit. Labels must be legible, durable, and correct.
items.evidence_protocol: Open 10% of electrical panels (minimum 5). For each: confirm every breaker is labeled with the circuit it controls (not blank, not unreadable pencil markings, not outdated). Test a sample of labels by turning off a breaker and confirming the equipment identified in the label actually de-energizes. Unlabeled breakers are a finding and a safety risk — workers may need to de-energize equipment in an emergency.

items.requirement: Ground fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs) must be used for personnel protection in specific locations: (1) all 120V single-phase 15A and 20A receptacle outlets in wet locations, construction areas, and outdoor receptacles; (2) temporary wiring on construction sites; (3) receptacles in bathrooms, kitchens, near sinks, and other wet areas.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of wet locations, outdoor outlets, and construction/maintenance areas (minimum 5 locations). Press the TEST button on each GFCI outlet or circuit breaker GFCI: the reset button should pop out or the breaker should trip. Then press RESET to restore power. Confirm GFCI protection is present on all outdoor receptacles and any receptacles within 6 feet of water sources (sinks, floor drains, hose connections). Note any non-GFCI outlets in required locations.

items.requirement: All electrical equipment must be properly grounded. Cord and plug connected equipment must be grounded through a grounding prong in the attachment plug. Fixed equipment must be grounded through the equipment grounding conductor in the supply circuit. Metal raceways, cable trays, and enclosures must be continuously and effectively grounded.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of portable power tools and extension cords (minimum 10 items). Confirm: three-prong plugs are intact (grounding prong not removed or bent); no two-prong adapters ('cheater plugs') are in use; the ground prong has not been cut or taped. For 10% of fixed equipment (minimum 5 machines): confirm the grounding conductor is connected at both the equipment and the panel.

items.requirement: Flexible cords and cables must be used only in permitted applications. Flexible cords must NOT be used: as a substitute for fixed wiring; run through holes in walls, ceilings, or floors; run through doorways, windows, or similar openings; attached to building surfaces; concealed behind building walls, ceilings, or floors.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of production, maintenance, and office areas (minimum 5 areas). Look specifically for: extension cords used as permanent wiring (plugged in and in continuous use for months or years); cords run through walls, under carpets, or through ceiling tiles; cords attached to building surfaces with staples, cable clips, or adhesive mounts; daisy-chained extension cords or power strips connected to other power strips. Each of these conditions is a finding.

items.requirement: Extension cords in use must be maintained in safe condition. Damaged cords must be immediately removed from service. Cords must be rated for the load they serve and must not be overloaded.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of extension cords in use (minimum 10 cords). For each: confirm no cuts, cracks, abraded insulation, or exposed conductors; all connectors are intact (no taped or makeshift repairs); the cord gauge (AWG) is appropriate for the load (a 16 AWG cord should not serve a high-draw tool). Any cord with damaged insulation or a makeshift repair must be removed from service immediately.

items.requirement: All junction boxes, outlet boxes, device boxes, and fittings must have approved covers. Open knockouts in boxes must be closed with appropriate fittings. No open spaces in electrical enclosures are permitted.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of electrical areas (minimum 5 areas) focusing specifically on junction boxes, conduit junction points, and panel knockouts. Confirm: all boxes have covers installed; open knockouts are closed; no wire bundles are entering panels or junction boxes without conduit or appropriate fittings; no gaping holes exist in electrical enclosures. Look specifically at conduit runs near areas subject to vibration — these are prone to fittings loosening.

items.requirement: Switchboards and panelboards must be located in clean, dry locations. The area must be free of easily ignitible material. Working space in front of panels must be kept clear. Panelboards must have a secured door or cover over the live parts.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of panelboard locations (minimum 5). Confirm: the panel is in a clean, dry location (not a janitorial closet, not in a wet process area without appropriate enclosure rating); no combustible materials (cardboard boxes, rags, cleaning supplies) are stored adjacent to or inside the panel area; the panel door is present, closes properly, and can be latched.

items.requirement: Overcurrent protection (circuit breakers and fuses) must be correctly sized for the conductor they protect. Breakers and fuses must not be modified, bypassed, or oversized to prevent tripping. Tandem breakers (two-circuit breakers in a single slot) must only be used in panels specifically designed to accept them.
items.evidence_protocol: During the panel inspections, observe whether any breakers appear to have been modified (taped handles, oversized fuses in fuseholders). Ask the maintenance manager whether any breakers have been replaced with higher-rated units because they kept tripping — this is a serious finding (the correct fix is to identify and correct the overload, not increase the breaker size). Confirm no fuse slots have been filled with anything other than the correct-rated fuse.

items.requirement: Electrical equipment installed in areas where ignitable concentrations of combustible dust may be present must be specifically rated and approved for that hazard classification. Gypsum dust (calcined hemihydrate) may be a Class II combustible dust depending on particle size and moisture content. Any area with significant gypsum dust accumulation requires a hazardous location assessment.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the facility's area classification study or hazardous location assessment. Confirm whether any areas have been classified as Class II Division 1 or Division 2 (combustible dust environments). If classified areas exist, confirm that all electrical equipment in those areas carries the appropriate listing (explosion-proof for Division 1; dust-ignition-proof or purged/pressurized for Division 2). Note: if no classification study has been performed but visible dust accumulations exist on electrical equipment in process areas, this warrants an immediate engineering review.

items.requirement: In classified hazardous locations, conduit seals must be installed at conduit entries into enclosures containing ignition-capable equipment. Seals prevent the passage of gases, vapors, or flames from one area to another through the conduit system.
items.evidence_protocol: If classified hazardous locations exist at this facility: walk to 10% of electrical enclosures in classified areas (minimum 5 locations). Confirm conduit seals are installed within 18 inches of each enclosure. Confirm seal fittings are filled (not empty shells — the sealing compound must be visible or confirmed). Unsealed conduit in a classified area is a significant explosion hazard.

items.requirement: Only qualified persons (those trained and knowledgeable in the construction and operation of electrical equipment and the hazards involved) may work on energized electrical parts of 50 volts or more. Unqualified persons must be kept at least 10 feet away from exposed energized parts of overhead lines.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the maintenance manager to identify which employees are designated as qualified electrical workers. Request training records for those individuals. Confirm their training covers: the electrical hazards present at this facility; selection and use of PPE (arc flash, insulated tools); approach distances to energized conductors; and emergency response for electrical incidents. Confirm non-qualified employees are not performing electrical work.

items.requirement: Electrical equipment must be de-energized before employees work on or near exposed live parts, unless de-energizing creates additional or increased hazards or is infeasible due to equipment design or operational continuity. When energized electrical work is performed, it must be done under an Energized Electrical Work Permit (EEWP) unless the work is classified as routine by a qualified person.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether the facility performs any energized electrical work (taking voltage readings, thermographic scanning, troubleshooting with power on). If yes, request the Energized Electrical Work Permit program and a sample of completed EEWPs. Confirm the permits identify the hazard (arc flash incident energy), the required PPE (arc flash suit rating), and justification for working energized. Confirm a qualified person approved each permit.

items.requirement: Personal protective equipment (PPE) for electrical work must include rubber insulating gloves rated for the voltage class, arc flash protection (arc-rated clothing, face shield) when arc flash hazard exists, and insulated tools.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the arc flash hazard analysis (AFHA) for this facility. Confirm the AFHA has been performed and updated within the past 5 years or following any significant electrical system change. Confirm PPE requirements are communicated to qualified electrical workers — confirm rubber insulating gloves, arc-rated FR clothing, and face shields are available and used. Inspect 10% of electrical PPE (minimum 3 sets): check glove test dates (every 6 months) and arc-rated garment condition.

items.requirement: Hand and portable powered tools and equipment must be maintained in a safe condition. Tools must be inspected before use and removed from service if defective.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to tool storage areas and active work areas. Inspect 10% of tools in service (minimum 10 tools). For each: look for cracked handles, broken guards, damaged power cords, missing insulation on insulated tools, dull or damaged cutting edges, and bent or deformed striking surfaces. Ask whether a formal tool inspection program exists and how damaged tools are removed from service. Confirm no defective tools are tagged as available for use.

items.requirement: Compressed air used for cleaning purposes must be reduced to less than 30 psi when the nozzle or orifice is dead-ended (i.e., the nozzle could be blocked against a surface and pressure could build up). The nozzle must also have side openings to prevent dead-end pressure buildup. If compressed air is used for personal cleaning, effective chip guarding and PPE must be used.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to areas where compressed air cleaning is performed. Confirm: OSHA-compliant safety nozzles (with side-vent holes) are used rather than standard open-ended air nozzles; if a pressure gauge is present on the line, confirm pressure is at or below 30 psi when the air gun is dead-ended. Ask employees whether they have ever blown compressed air on themselves or their clothing to clean off dust — this practice is prohibited and indicates a training gap.

items.requirement: Portable electrically-powered hand tools (drills, grinders, saws) must be grounded through a three-prong plug and grounding conductor, OR be double-insulated. Double-insulated tools must be distinctly marked and must not be used in wet environments.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of portable electric tools (minimum 10). For each: confirm the power cord has a three-prong grounding plug (or the tool is marked as double-insulated); the grounding prong is not bent, removed, or damaged; no adapter plugs are in use. For double-insulated tools: confirm they are clearly marked 'Double Insulated' on the tool body and are not being used in wet conditions.

items.requirement: Portable circular saws must be equipped with a fixed guard above the base plate and a self-adjusting lower guard that returns to the covering position when removed from the material. The lower guard must not be pinned or wedged open.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of portable circular saws at the facility. For each: confirm the upper fixed guard is present; lift the saw and confirm the lower guard returns automatically to cover the blade when the saw is not cutting; confirm the lower guard is not modified to stay open. Ask maintenance employees whether they have ever been tempted to pin back the lower guard — this practice is common and must be specifically addressed in training.

items.requirement: Portable belt sanders must be provided with guards at each nip point where the sanding belt makes contact with the pulley. Guards must be installed to prevent contact with nip points.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of portable belt sanders. For each: confirm nip-point guards are present at the drive pulley and at the idler pulley where the belt makes contact. Confirm guards are firmly attached. Ask operators to demonstrate proper grip and operating technique.

items.requirement: Switches on portable circular saws, chain saws, and percussion tools must be designed so the tool will stop running when pressure is removed from the switch. These tools must not be capable of being locked in the 'on' position.
items.evidence_protocol: Test 10% of portable circular saws (unplugged/unloaded). Squeeze the trigger and release it — confirm the blade stops when the trigger is released. Confirm there is no mechanism to lock the trigger in the running position. If a locking mechanism exists on any tool in this category, that tool must be removed from service.

items.requirement: Pneumatic tools must be secured to hoses to prevent accidental disconnection. Safety clips or retainers must be used on all pneumatic chisels, hammers, drills, and similar tools to prevent the tool from being ejected from the barrel.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to areas where pneumatic tools are used. Inspect 10% of pneumatic tools and hose connections (minimum 5). Confirm: safety clips (also called retaining devices or whip checks) are installed at each hose-to-tool and hose-to-hose connection; the safety clip prevents the tool from ejecting if pressure is accidentally released; the clip is in good condition and properly engaged.

items.requirement: Pneumatic hose connections must be secured with positive means to prevent accidental disconnection. Safety locking pins, wire, or safety cables must be used at each hose coupling in lines over 1/2 inch inside diameter.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of pneumatic hose runs (minimum 5 runs). Confirm positive-locking hose couplings are used at each connection point, or that safety wires/whip-check cables are attached at each joint. Trace each hose run from the tool to the manifold — confirm every connection point is secured. Pay particular attention to overhead hose runs where a disconnected hose could flail and injure workers below.

items.requirement: Abrasive wheels used on portable grinders must be used only on machines provided with safety guards — except for wheels where the application makes guarding impractical (disc wheels used for grinding inside grooves) where the entire grinding surface is engaged.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of portable grinders (angle grinders, die grinders, right-angle grinders). For each: confirm the wheel guard is present and covers the required arc of the wheel; the guard is firmly attached and cannot be removed without tools; the guard has not been bent back, removed, or replaced with a makeshift cover. Guards removed for convenience is the most common violation — look for any grinder with the guard missing.

items.requirement: Abrasive wheels on portable grinders must be closely inspected and ring-tested (sounded) immediately before mounting. The wheel must fit freely on the spindle; flanges must be the correct size; and the wheel must not exceed the RPM rating of the grinder.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the person responsible for changing grinding wheels on portable grinders to demonstrate the ring test (tap the wheel with a non-metallic object 1-2 inches from the edge — a clear ring indicates no cracks; a dull thud indicates a cracked wheel). Inspect 10% of wheels currently mounted on portable grinders (minimum 5): confirm the maximum RPM rating on the wheel equals or exceeds the no-load RPM of the grinder it is mounted on. A wheel rated for less than the grinder's speed is a fragmentation hazard.

items.requirement: Explosive-actuated fastening tools (powder-actuated tools) must be provided with specific safety features: a guarded muzzle that prevents tool discharge unless against a surface; a safety that prevents firing unless the muzzle guard is depressed; a firing mechanism that prevents discharge if dropped.
items.evidence_protocol: If powder-actuated tools are used at this facility: confirm all tools have muzzle guards and active safety mechanisms. Test the safety by attempting to fire the tool with the muzzle not depressed against a surface (with an unloaded tool) — it must not fire. Confirm tools are stored unloaded in a locked case when not in use.

items.requirement: Operators of explosive-actuated fastening tools must be trained to operate them safely and must carry their certification on their person at all times while using the tool. Operators must be at least 18 years old.
items.evidence_protocol: If powder-actuated tools are used: request training/certification records for all current operators. Confirm training was conducted by the tool manufacturer or an authorized trainer. Ask an operator to produce their certification card — they must have it on their person while using the tool. Confirm no operators are under 18 years of age.

items.requirement: Jacks (hydraulic, mechanical, and ratchet-type) must have the rated load legibly and permanently marked in a prominent location.
