Jacks must not be loaded beyond their rated capacity. The base of each jack must be placed on a firm, level surface. All load-bearing surfaces must be protected against slippage.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of floor jacks, bottle jacks, and service jacks at the facility. For each: confirm the rated capacity is marked on the jack body; no jack shows evidence of use beyond its rated capacity (bent frame, damaged seals, hydraulic fluid leaks); when in use, safety stands must be in place to support the load if the jack fails. Ask employees whether they ever leave loads supported only by jacks without safety stands underneath.

items.requirement: A written Hazard Communication (HazCom) program must be developed, implemented, and maintained at the workplace. The program must describe how the facility will meet the requirements for labels, SDSs, and employee training, and must include a list of all hazardous chemicals at the workplace.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written HazCom program. Confirm: (1) a current revision date within the past 12 months or after any significant chemical inventory change; (2) the program addresses all three implementation areas — labels, SDSs, and training; (3) a chemical inventory list is attached or referenced, and is current. Ask how new chemicals are added to the inventory — confirm a process exists before chemicals arrive on site.

items.requirement: The HazCom program must be made available to employees and their representatives upon request.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the written program is accessible to all employees — not locked in a manager's file cabinet. Ask an employee where they would find the HazCom program — they should be able to identify a location (safety office, shared drive, intranet, binder at workstation).

items.requirement: Multi-employer workplaces: where employees of other employers work on site, the host employer must ensure those contractors receive SDSs and labeling information for hazardous chemicals they may be exposed to.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether contractors regularly work at this facility. If yes, confirm the site has a process to share SDSs and HazCom information before contractors begin work — check contractor safety orientation documentation or pre-job meeting records for HazCom information exchange.

items.requirement: Each container of hazardous chemicals in the workplace must be labeled, tagged, or marked with: (1) product identifier; (2) signal word; (3) hazard statement(s); (4) pictogram(s); (5) precautionary statement(s); and (6) name/address/phone number of the chemical manufacturer, importer, or responsible party. Labels must be legible, in English, and prominently displayed.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of chemical use and storage areas (minimum 5 areas). For each container in the area: confirm the GHS-compliant label is present and all six required elements are readable. Look specifically for: unlabeled containers, labels covered by tape or stickers, faded or worn labels, secondary containers filled from a larger container and not re-labeled. Confirm labels have not been removed or defaced.

items.requirement: Workplace labels on secondary containers (re-filled from a bulk container) must contain, at minimum, the product identifier and words, pictures, symbols, or combinations that provide general information about the hazards. Full GHS labels are preferred.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of secondary containers at the facility (spray bottles, small jugs, portable tanks — minimum 10 containers). Confirm each has at minimum the product name/identifier and a general hazard indication. Any secondary container with only a handwritten name but no hazard information is deficient.

items.requirement: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) must be maintained for each hazardous chemical used at the workplace. SDSs must be in the revised 16-section GHS format (not the old 8-section MSDS format). Each SDS must be in English and must be accessible to employees in their work area during each work shift.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the SDS library/binder for 10% of work areas (minimum 3 areas). Spot-check 10% of chemicals in the inventory (minimum 10 chemicals): confirm an SDS exists for each chemical; confirm the SDS is in the 16-section GHS format (not the older MSDS format); confirm the SDS is current (check the revision date — if a chemical has changed formulation, the SDS must reflect that). Note any chemicals in use that lack an SDS.

items.requirement: SDSs must contain the required 16 sections with accurate, complete information. Critical sections include: Section 2 (hazard identification — signal word, pictograms, hazard statements); Section 4 (first aid); Section 6 (accidental release); Section 7 (handling/storage); Section 8 (exposure controls/PPE); Section 11 (toxicological information).
items.evidence_protocol: Pull SDSs for 10% of chemicals in the highest-hazard areas (minimum 5 SDSs). Review each for completeness: Section 2 must include the signal word and GHS hazard statements; Section 8 must specify PPE requirements that match what employees are actually using; Section 4 must describe first aid steps. If any critical section states 'not applicable' or is clearly incomplete for a chemical with known hazards, document as a finding.

items.requirement: SDSs must be readily accessible to employees on all shifts and in all work areas where hazardous chemicals are used. Electronic access is acceptable only if there are no barriers to immediate access and a backup system (printed copies) is available for power outages.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask employees in 10% of work areas (minimum 5 employees) how they would access an SDS for a chemical they work with if they needed it right now. Time the response if electronic — confirm access is immediate and not dependent on finding a supervisor. If electronic-only, ask what happens if the computer is down.

items.requirement:
Employees must be trained on HazCom at the time of initial assignment and when a new chemical hazard is introduced. Training must cover the following required topics:
(1) The requirements of the HazCom standard and employee rights under it
(2) Operations in the work area where hazardous chemicals are present
(3) Location and availability of the written HazCom program, chemical inventory, and SDSs
(4) Methods used to detect the presence or release of hazardous chemicals
(5) Physical, health, simple asphyxiation, combustible dust, and pyrophoric gas hazards of chemicals
(6) Measures employees can take to protect themselves
(7) Details of the labeling system and how to interpret GHS labels and SDSs
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — Request training records for 10% of employees who work with or near hazardous chemicals (minimum 10 employees). Confirm initial training before first exposure and retraining when new hazards are introduced. Confirm training covers all 7 required topics above. Interview 10% of those employees (minimum 5):
□ Can they identify the signal word and at least one hazard statement on a chemical label in their area?
□ Can they locate the SDS for that chemical?
□ Do they know what PPE to use?
□ Can they describe what to do in case of a spill?
Gaps between training records and actual employee knowledge are a finding.

items.requirement: Combustible dust hazards must be addressed in the HazCom program. Chemical manufacturers and employers must classify combustible dust hazards and communicate those hazards via labels and SDSs. For gypsum manufacturing, calcined gypsum (stucco/hemihydrate) and fine limestone dust may present combustible dust hazards depending on particle size and moisture content.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether the facility has conducted a combustible dust hazard assessment (NFPA 652 Process Hazard Analysis or equivalent). Review the HazCom program and SDSs for raw gypsum, calcined gypsum, and any other dust-generating processes — confirm combustible dust hazards are identified where applicable. Ask whether dust accumulation on elevated surfaces is monitored and cleaned — accumulations greater than 1/32 inch over 5% of a room's floor area is a National Fire Protection Association benchmark for hazardous accumulation.

items.requirement: Handling and storage of materials must be conducted safely to minimize risks of physical injury. Storage areas must prevent tripping, fire, and pest harborage. Materials must not be stored in a way that creates hazards from overloading floors or blocking exits, emergency equipment, or passageways.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk 10% of storage areas (minimum 5). Confirm: aisles and passageways are clear; materials are stacked safely and will not tip; exits, fire extinguishers, and emergency equipment are not blocked; floors are not overloaded relative to their rated capacity. Note any materials stored against electrical panels or within the required working space.

items.requirement: Powered industrial trucks (forklifts, pallet jacks, reach trucks, etc.) must meet ASME B56.1 design requirements. Each truck must carry a nameplate showing its rated capacity, type designation, and applicable attachments.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of powered industrial trucks (minimum 5 trucks). For each: confirm the nameplate is present and legible; the type designation (E, ES, EE, EX, G, GS, LP, LPS, D, DS, DY) is visible; the rated capacity is shown and matches the loads being carried. A truck with a missing or unreadable nameplate must be taken out of service until the designation is re-established.

items.requirement: Only approved trucks of the correct type designation may be used in classified locations (areas with flammable vapors, gases, or combustible dust). The atmosphere must be classified before trucks are assigned to work in those areas.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether any areas where powered industrial trucks operate are classified as hazardous (flammable vapors, combustible dust). If yes, confirm the truck type designation matches the location classification. For a gypsum manufacturing facility, areas with calcined gypsum dust may require a dust-rated truck depending on the classification study.

items.requirement: Safe operating procedures must be followed at all times. Required procedures include: travel with forks in lowered position; observe posted speed limits; maintain safe distances from personnel; yield to pedestrians; no riding on the forks or load; no placing arms or legs between the uprights or outside the running lines of the truck.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe powered industrial truck operations for a minimum of 15 minutes in 10% of operational areas (minimum 2 areas). Note any deviations from safe operating practices. Interview 10% of operators (minimum 3): ask what the maximum travel speed is, what they do when approaching a blind corner, and what the proper fork height is during travel. Check whether pedestrian/forklift traffic is separated — shared travel lanes without physical separation or floor markings are a finding.

items.requirement: Fueling, battery charging, and LPG cylinder exchange must be conducted in designated areas. The area must be clear of ignition sources, well-ventilated, and equipped with appropriate fire protection. Battery charging areas must have spill protection, water for flushing, and eye wash equipment.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to the battery charging area and/or LPG cylinder exchange area. Confirm: the area is designated and posted; no smoking signs are posted and enforced; open flames are prohibited; adequate ventilation prevents hydrogen gas accumulation in the charging area (hydrogen is released during battery charging); an eye wash station is within 10 seconds of the battery charging area; spill containment or a floor drain is present for battery acid.

items.requirement: Powered industrial trucks must not be operated in areas where the floor or surface cannot safely support the truck and its load. Bridge plates, dock boards, and loading ramps must be checked before the truck crosses them.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask operators how they determine whether a surface can support the truck and its load. Confirm dock boards are secured before a forklift crosses them. Ask whether any areas of the facility have floor loading limitations that are communicated to operators.

items.requirement: Powered industrial trucks must be examined before being placed in service each day (or shift, if used around the clock). Pre-shift inspections must check all safety components. Defects found before or during use must be reported immediately; trucks must not be used until the defect is corrected.
items.evidence_protocol: Request pre-shift inspection records for 10% of trucks for the past 30 days (minimum 3 trucks, 5 days each). Confirm: inspections are conducted each shift; all required items are checked (horn, lights, brakes, steering, forks, mast, hydraulics, tires, battery/fuel, overhead guard, load backrest); defects found on any inspection log are traced to a corrective maintenance record before the truck was returned to service.

items.requirement: Industrial trucks must be maintained in safe operating condition at all times. Only trained personnel may perform maintenance. Trucks must not be used with mechanical defects. Fuel and hydraulic systems must not have leaks.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe 10% of trucks (minimum 3 trucks). Check for: hydraulic fluid leaks (staining under the mast or hose connections); LPG hose cracking or chafing; fork damage (bent tines, cracked welds at the shank); overhead guard condition; operational lights and horn. Attempt to test the horn on each truck. Ask the maintenance coordinator when the last periodic maintenance was performed and request the service log.

items.requirement: All powered industrial truck operators must be trained before operating a truck. Training must consist of a combination of formal instruction, practical training, and evaluation. The training program must cover truck-specific topics AND workplace-specific topics per 29 CFR 1910.178(l)(2). See Evidence column for required training elements.
items.evidence_protocol:
CHECKLIST — Request operator training and certification records for 100% of currently active operators. Confirm each record includes formal instruction, practical training on the specific truck type, and a workplace-specific evaluation. Required training topics must include:
□ Truck-specific: controls, instrumentation, steering, maneuvering, visibility, fork/attachment operation, refueling/charging, pre-use inspection, limitations of the truck
□ Workplace-specific: surface conditions, load composition/stability, load manipulation/stacking, pedestrian traffic, narrow aisles, hazardous locations, ramps/slopes, closed environments and ventilation
Each operator must have a certification card or record showing the specific truck type(s) they are qualified to operate.

items.requirement: Refresher training and re-evaluation must be conducted when: an operator is observed operating the truck unsafely; an operator has been in an accident or near-miss; the operator is assigned to a different type of truck; conditions in the workplace change that affect safe operation; or the operator has not operated a truck for more than 90 days.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the safety manager for a log of forklift incidents, near-misses, and observed unsafe acts in the past 12 months. For each event: confirm refresher training was documented for the involved operator before they returned to operation. Confirm operators returning after extended absences (90+ days without operating a truck) are re-evaluated before resuming operations.

items.requirement: The employer must certify that each operator has been trained and evaluated. The certification must include the operator's name, the date of training, the date of evaluation, and the identity of the person performing the training or evaluation.
items.evidence_protocol: Review 10% of operator certification records (minimum 5 records). Confirm each record contains all four required elements: operator name, training date, evaluation date, and trainer/evaluator identity. A training certificate that shows only the operator name and date — without an evaluation date and trainer identity — is incomplete.

items.requirement: Overhead and gantry cranes must be designed in accordance with ASME B30.2 and must be in good mechanical condition. All safety devices, warning systems, and limit switches must be functional.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm each overhead/gantry crane at the facility has documentation of design compliance (nameplate, engineering drawings, or original installation certification). Walk to 10% of cranes (minimum all cranes if fewer than 5; otherwise minimum 5). For each: visually inspect for obvious structural damage (bent girders, cracked welds, corroded hook blocks); test the hoist limit switch at the start of the operator's shift; confirm the warning device (bell or horn) functions.

items.requirement: All cranes must display the rated load capacity in a prominent location. The operator must not exceed the rated load. Rated load capacities must reflect any attachments or configuration changes.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm rated load markings are visible on 10% of cranes (minimum all cranes if fewer than 5). Ask operators how they verify a load does not exceed the crane's capacity. Confirm no makeshift indicators (tape, paint marks) have been substituted for proper load markings.

items.requirement: Cranes in regular service must undergo frequent inspections (daily to monthly, depending on use) and periodic inspections (1-12 months, depending on use). Specific items requiring frequent inspection include: all functional operating mechanisms for maladjustment; deterioration or leakage in lines, tanks, valves, drain pumps; hooks; hoist chains and end connections; rope reeving for non-compliance.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the crane inspection log for each overhead crane for the past 12 months. Confirm: daily/frequent inspections are documented for each day the crane is used; the inspection covers all required components; and any deficiencies found are documented and traced to a corrective action before the crane was returned to service. A crane operated without a documented pre-use inspection is a finding.

items.requirement: Periodic inspections (at 1-12 month intervals depending on use) must cover all components not covered in frequent inspections, including deformed, cracked, or corroded members; loose bolts or rivets; cracked or worn sheaves and drums; worn, cracked, or distorted parts; all hooks per ASME B30.10.
items.evidence_protocol: Request periodic inspection records for all overhead cranes. Confirm the inspections were performed by a qualified person (documented competency) at the required interval for the crane's service class. Confirm the inspection covered structural members, hooks, rope, brakes, limit switches, and electrical systems. Any crane without a current periodic inspection record must be taken out of service.

items.requirement: All wire ropes on cranes must be inspected and must be replaced if any of the following conditions exist: in running ropes — more than 2 broken wires in one lay in areas subject to normal abrasion, more than 1 broken wire at end attachments; visible wear reducing the rope diameter by 1/3 of the original diameter; kinking, crushing, bird-caging; evidence of heat damage; reduction from original diameter of 3/64 inch (1/2 inch rope) or more.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect wire rope on 10% of crane hoist systems (minimum all if fewer than 5; otherwise 5 rope inspections). Run a hand (with gloves) along accessible rope length looking for broken wires, kinks, or bird-caging. Measure rope diameter at multiple points with a rope gauge — compare to the original diameter specification. Confirm the rope inspection is documented in the periodic inspection record.

items.requirement: Crane operators must be qualified and must follow established operating procedures. Key requirements: do not use a crane to lift people; do not leave a load suspended unattended; protect the load from swinging; use tag lines to control loads; do not side-pull or drag loads; use proper hand signals or radio communication with the signal person.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe crane operations for 15 minutes at 10% of active cranes (minimum 1 observation). Confirm: operators are qualified (training/certification on file); proper signals are used (standardized hand signals or radio per ASME B30.2); no employees are under a suspended load; loads appear stable and within capacity. Ask operators when they would refuse to operate the crane.

items.requirement: A preventive maintenance (PM) program must be established based on the crane manufacturer's recommendations. Unsafe conditions found during any inspection must be corrected before the crane is returned to service. Cranes must not be loaded beyond their rated loads, and modifications that might affect safe operation require manufacturer or qualified engineer approval.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the crane PM schedule and records for the past 12 months. Confirm PM was performed at the manufacturer-recommended intervals. Request any records of crane modifications (extended booms, added attachments, structural repairs) and confirm manufacturer or qualified engineer approval was obtained.

items.requirement: All slings and attachments must be inspected before each use and removed from service if any of the following defects are present: missing or illegible identification tag; deformations, nicks, cracks, corrosion; weld splatter; worn or damaged end fittings; broken wires exceeding limits; kinking, crushing, or bird-caging in wire rope; cuts, tears, or holes in synthetic slings.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of slings in use (minimum 10 slings — include chain, wire rope, and synthetic web if all three types are present). For each: confirm identification markings are present and legible (rated capacity, type, manufacturer); look for damage conditions listed above; confirm no field repairs have been made (slings must be repaired by the manufacturer, not field-welded or patched). Confirm a daily inspection is performed before each use.

items.requirement: Sling capacity must not be exceeded. Rated capacity is based on the type of hitch (single leg, two-leg, basket, choker), the angle of lift, and the sling material. Using a sling at an angle reduces its effective capacity — a 60-degree sling angle reduces capacity to 87% of vertical capacity; a 30-degree angle reduces capacity to 50%.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask riggers to demonstrate their knowledge of sling angle capacity reduction. Confirm the rigging crew has a sling capacity chart available and uses it when configuring lifts. For any lifts observed, estimate the sling angle and confirm the apparent load is within capacity at that angle. Confirm synthetic web slings are not being used at temperatures above their rated maximum (typically 180°F for nylon).

items.requirement: Alloy steel chain slings must not be used below the minimum temperature limit for the grade of chain. Chain slings must be proof tested per ASME B30.9 requirements. Damaged chain slings must be removed from service and returned to the manufacturer for repair — field repairs by welding, cutting, or splicing are prohibited.
items.evidence_protocol: If chain slings are in use: inspect 10% of chain slings (minimum 3 slings). Confirm: the rated capacity tag is attached and legible; there are no bent, twisted, stretched, or cracked links; the hooks are not deformed or have more than 15% throat opening increase; the sling has been proof-tested (verify the test certificate or manufacturer's documentation). Confirm no chain slings have been repaired in the field.

items.requirement: Each employer must conduct a visual inspection of compressed gas cylinders to verify safety of use. Only cylinders that are in safe condition may be used. Safety relief devices must be inspected at regular intervals.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of compressed gas cylinder storage and use locations (minimum 3 locations). For each cylinder: visually inspect for corrosion, dents, damage to the valve, or missing valve cap; confirm the cylinder is properly identified with the gas name (not just a color code); check the hydrostatic retest date stamped on the cylinder — cylinders past their retest date must be removed from service. Confirm safety relief devices (pressure relief valves) are present and not blocked.

items.requirement: Compressed gas cylinders must be stored, handled, and used safely. Cylinders must be secured upright to prevent falling, stored away from heat sources, and kept away from electrical panels and ignition sources. Valve protection caps must be in place when cylinders are not connected.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of compressed gas cylinders at the facility (minimum 10 cylinders). Confirm: each cylinder is secured with a chain, strap, or bracket to prevent falling; valve protection caps are on all cylinders not in active use; cylinders are not stored near heat sources or open flames; full and empty cylinders are stored separately and labeled; oxygen cylinders are stored at least 20 feet from fuel gas cylinders (or separated by a 5-foot noncombustible barrier with a 30-minute fire rating).

items.requirement: When using compressed gas cylinders, cylinders must be moved properly (on an approved hand truck or cart), must not be dragged, dropped, or used as rollers. Gas must be withdrawn from cylinders only through listed regulators. Cylinders must not be emptied below 25 psig residual pressure.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask employees who use compressed gas cylinders to demonstrate how they move a cylinder. Confirm carts or hand trucks are available and used. Ask what they do when a cylinder runs out — confirm they do not drain the cylinder below residual pressure. Inspect 10% of cylinder carts (minimum 3): confirm carts are rated for the cylinder weight and have a chain or strap for securing.

items.requirement: Warning signs must be posted in compressed gas container storage areas. Storage areas for flammable gases must be free of ignition sources. Toxic and corrosive gases must be stored in a ventilated area with appropriate hazard identification.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to each compressed gas storage area. Confirm: DANGER or WARNING signs are posted indicating the hazardous gas present; for flammable gas storage — NO SMOKING and NO OPEN FLAMES signs are posted and any electrical equipment is rated for the hazardous location; for toxic gas storage — ventilation is provided; for oxidizer storage (oxygen) — no combustible materials are present in the storage area.

items.requirement: Flammable liquids must be stored in listed safety containers or approved storage cabinets. Quantities stored outside of approved storage areas must be limited. Safety cans must have a spring-closing lid, flash arrester, and must be labeled with contents.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of areas where flammable liquids are used or stored (minimum 3 areas). For each: confirm flammable liquids are stored in listed safety cans (UL or FM listed) or in approved storage cabinets; safety cans have working spring-closing lids and flash arresters; containers are labeled; quantities outside of storage cabinets do not exceed one-day or one-shift supply (generally 25 gallons for Class I liquids outside a storage room).

items.requirement: Storage cabinets for flammable liquids must meet design and capacity requirements: not more than 60 gallons of Class I or Class II liquids, or not more than 120 gallons of Class III liquids per cabinet; must be labeled FLAMMABLE — KEEP FIRE AWAY; must be liquid-tight at the bottom sill.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 100% of flammable liquid storage cabinets at the facility. For each: confirm the cabinet is labeled; measure or estimate the contents — confirm the 60-gallon Class I/II limit is not exceeded; confirm the bottom of the cabinet has a 2-inch sill or equivalent containment; confirm the cabinet doors close and latch properly.

items.requirement: Sources of ignition must be controlled in areas where flammable liquids are handled or stored. This includes open flames, smoking, welding, cutting, grinding, and electrical sparks. Grounding and bonding must be used during transfer of Class I flammable liquids to prevent static discharge.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to flammable liquid handling areas. Confirm: NO SMOKING signs are posted; no open flames or hot work is performed without a hot work permit; bonding cables and grounding clips are available for and used during flammable liquid transfers (confirm a grounding wire connects the dispensing container to the receiving container and both are grounded to earth). Ask whether hot work permits are required — confirm the permit system includes a check for proximity to flammable materials.

items.requirement: Adequate housekeeping must be maintained in flammable liquid storage and use areas. Waste materials and rags soaked with flammable or combustible liquids must be stored in metal containers with self-closing lids and disposed of properly.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of areas where flammable liquids are used (minimum 3 areas). Look for: oily or solvent-soaked rags left on surfaces or in cardboard boxes; spills not cleaned up; flammable waste liquids stored in unapproved containers (plastic bottles, glass jars). Confirm metal waste rag cans with self-closing lids are present and in use in areas where solvent-soaked rags are generated.

items.requirement: LP gas equipment and systems must be approved for the intended use. Containers and cylinders must be marked with the type of gas, water capacity, and service pressure. Containers must not exceed their rated fill density.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether LP gas is used at this facility (forklifts, heating, process). If yes: inspect 10% of LP gas containers (minimum 3 containers). Confirm: DOT or ASME markings are present; the tare weight and water capacity are marked on DOT cylinders; cylinder valves and connections are in good condition (no leaks — check with a soap solution or combustible gas detector); over-flow prevention valves are present on DOT cylinders.

items.requirement: LP gas storage areas must be kept clear of ignitable materials and weeds/debris. LP gas must not be used in buildings unless the building is ventilated to prevent dangerous concentrations. Cylinders must be stored in ventilated areas away from ignition sources and must not be stored inside buildings except as permitted.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to LP gas cylinder storage and use areas. Confirm: storage area is outdoors or in a ventilated area; cylinders are not stored inside heated buildings (other than as specifically permitted for direct use); the area is clear of combustible vegetation, debris, and materials within 10 feet; posted NO SMOKING and NO OPEN FLAMES signs are visible; electrical equipment in the storage area is appropriate for the location classification.

items.requirement: Maintenance and operational personnel who handle LP gas equipment must be properly trained in safe handling, storage, and use. Emergency shutoffs must be clearly marked and readily accessible.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm LP gas training records for employees who handle LP gas cylinders or operate LP-fueled equipment (forklift operators, maintenance staff). Confirm at least one trained person per shift. Walk to LP gas system shutoffs — confirm they are clearly labeled, accessible, and operable without special tools. Ask an employee where the emergency shutoff is and have them point to it.

items.requirement: Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) must be provided where dust, fumes, mist, vapors, or gases from grinding, abrasive blasting, spray finishing, or other processes create health or fire hazards. Ventilation systems must be designed to maintain contaminant levels below applicable PELs.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of operations with identified air contaminant sources (grinding, blending, material handling, spray finishing, machining — minimum 3 operations). Confirm: LEV hoods or enclosures are present and positioned at each emission source; fans/blowers are operational (test by holding a tissue or smoke pencil near the hood); ductwork is not damaged, disconnected, or obstructed; dust collectors are operational and maintained.

items.requirement: Exhaust ventilation systems must have suitable dust collectors or other cleaning devices to prevent discharge of dangerous concentrations of contaminants to the outdoor air or to areas where personnel are present. Dust collector hoppers must be emptied and cleaned at appropriate intervals.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of dust collection systems (minimum 3 systems). For each: confirm the collection device (baghouse, cartridge filter, cyclone) is operating; clean or replace filter indicators show the filter is not plugged; the dust hopper is not overflowing; the collected material is being removed at appropriate intervals and disposed of safely. For gypsum dust collectors — confirm accumulated dust is not creating a combustible dust accumulation hazard.

items.requirement: Where spray finishing operations are conducted, they must be performed in a spray booth or spray room. The spray area must be ventilated, free of ignition sources, and equipped with appropriate fire protection.
items.evidence_protocol: If spray finishing is conducted at this facility: confirm it is performed in a listed spray booth or spray room; the ventilation is operational and provides adequate air velocity across the work surface (generally 100 fpm); the area is free of open flames and non-explosion-proof electrical equipment; overspray and residues are cleaned at regular intervals.

items.requirement: When employees are exposed to noise at or above an 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA) of 85 dBA (Action Level), the employer must implement a continuing, effective hearing conservation program (HCP).
items.evidence_protocol: Request the facility's noise exposure monitoring records or industrial hygiene survey. Confirm noise monitoring has been conducted in areas where employees may be exposed at or above 85 dBA (production areas, compressor rooms, grinding operations). If no monitoring has been conducted and the facility has production equipment — document as a finding, as a manufacturing facility is highly likely to have employees exceeding the 85 dBA action level.

items.requirement: Noise monitoring must be conducted whenever there is indication that an employee's noise exposure may equal or exceed the 85 dBA action level. Monitoring results must be representative of employee noise exposures and must be repeated when production changes, process changes, or equipment changes may affect noise levels.
items.evidence_protocol: Request noise monitoring records including monitoring dates, areas monitored, equipment used, employee dosimetry results, and dates of last re-monitoring. Confirm monitoring was repeated after any significant equipment additions or facility changes. Confirm employees notified of monitoring results have access to their individual exposure data.

items.requirement: Audiometric testing must be established and maintained for all employees whose noise exposures equal or exceed the 85 dBA action level. Tests must include a baseline audiogram within 6 months of first exposure and annual audiograms thereafter.
items.evidence_protocol: Request audiometric testing records for 10% of employees in noise-exposed areas (minimum 10 employees). Confirm: a baseline audiogram was obtained within 6 months of first exposure (or prior to exposure if OATS is used); annual audiograms are on file for each year of employment in a noise-exposed area; the testing program is conducted by a licensed or certified audiologist, physician, or qualified technician using a calibrated audiometer.

items.requirement: When an audiogram reveals a Standard Threshold Shift (STS) — an average change in hearing threshold of 10 dB or more at 2000, 3000, and 4000 Hz — follow-up steps must be taken: retest within 30 days; refer to audiologist if the shift is persistent; fit with hearing protectors if not already using them; re-evaluate the hearing protectors if already being used.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the audiometric program administrator what the procedure is when an STS is identified. Request records of any STS cases in the past 3 years and confirm: the employee was notified in writing within 21 days; follow-up actions were documented; if hearing protection was already being used, the adequacy of the protector was re-evaluated.

items.requirement: Hearing protectors must be made available at no cost to all employees exposed at or above the action level (85 dBA TWA). Employees exposed at or above the PEL (90 dBA TWA) must use hearing protectors. Hearing protectors must provide adequate attenuation for the noise level.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of high-noise areas (minimum 3 areas). Confirm: hearing protectors are available and in use; the type and Noise Reduction Rating (NRR) of the hearing protectors is appropriate for the noise level in that area (at minimum, the NRR must reduce effective exposure below 90 dBA). Interview 10% of employees in noise-exposed areas (minimum 5): ask whether hearing protectors are available, how they know which type to use, and whether they wear them consistently.

items.requirement: A training program must be established for each employee whose exposures equal or exceed the 85 dBA action level. Training must be conducted annually and must cover: the effects of noise on hearing; the purpose, advantages, and limitations of hearing protectors; the purpose of audiometric testing; and the requirements of the standard.
items.evidence_protocol: Request hearing conservation training records for 10% of noise-exposed employees (minimum 10 employees). Confirm: initial training was conducted before first exposure; annual retraining is documented; training covers all four required content areas. Interview 10% of trained employees (minimum 5): ask what the effects of long-term noise exposure are, what their NRR rating means, and when audiometric testing is required.

items.requirement: Noise exposure monitoring records, audiometric test results, background sound pressure levels used for audiometric testing, and hearing conservation program records must be retained for the duration of the employee's employment plus 3 years. Audiometric test results are retained for the duration of employment.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm record retention protocols — noise monitoring records for minimum 2 years; audiometric records for duration of employment. Confirm records are accessible to current employees and former employees upon request. Ask whether records for terminated employees are maintained for the required period.

items.requirement: Medical personnel must be available to provide advice and consultation on matters of occupational health. At a minimum, the employer must have a formal relationship (physician, occupational health clinic, or licensed health professional) for referral and consultation.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the facility has an identified occupational health provider or physician. Request the contract, letter of agreement, or documented relationship. Confirm the provider is familiar with the specific hazards at this facility (not just a general practitioner with no knowledge of the work environment).

items.requirement: In the absence of an infirmary, clinic, or hospital near the workplace that can be used for the treatment of injured employees, a person or persons must be adequately trained to render first aid. Adequate first aid supplies must be maintained and readily available.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether a hospital, clinic, or infirmary is within a reasonable distance of this facility (typically considered 3-4 minutes travel for life-threatening injuries). If not: confirm the number of employees per shift who are trained and certified first aid responders; confirm at least one first aid-trained person is present on every shift; request current CPR/first aid certification records for all designated responders. Confirm first aid supplies are present, accessible, and within their expiration dates.

items.requirement: First aid kits must be adequate for the hazards present at the facility. Contents must be tailored to the types of injuries that could occur and must be replaced after use. The kit must be inspected regularly.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect 10% of first aid kits (minimum 3 kits, including at least one remote/satellite kit far from the main first aid station). For each: confirm an inventory list is present; confirm all items on the inventory list are present and within expiration dates; confirm the kit has not been used and not restocked (check for empty wrappers or obviously depleted supplies); confirm the kit includes supplies appropriate for the chemical and physical hazards at this facility (e.g., burn gel if thermal burns are a hazard; chemical neutralizer if corrosive exposures are possible).

items.requirement: Where the eyes or body of any person may be exposed to injurious corrosive materials, suitable facilities for quick drenching or flushing of the eyes and body must be provided within the work area for immediate emergency use.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of areas where corrosive chemicals (acids, caustics, strong oxidizers, chemical concentrates) are handled (minimum all such areas if fewer than 5). Confirm: an emergency eyewash station and/or safety shower is present within the immediate work area (10-second unobstructed travel distance per ANSI Z358.1); the path to the unit is clear and unobstructed; the unit is not locked or behind a barrier. For highly corrosive materials, confirm a combination eyewash/shower unit rather than an eyewash-only unit.

items.requirement: Emergency eyewash and shower units must be located on the same level as the hazard, with an unobstructed path requiring no more than 10 seconds of travel (approximately 55 feet for walking speed). Units must be in areas that are well-lit and conspicuously identified with a highly visible sign.
items.evidence_protocol: For each eyewash/shower unit in the sample: (1) measure or pace the distance from the identified hazard to the unit — confirm it is within 55 feet on the same level; (2) time the travel from the hazard to the unit — confirm it can be reached in 10 seconds or less; (3) confirm the path requires no doors that could obstruct a person with impaired vision; (4) confirm the unit is identified with a visible, illuminated or highly contrasted sign.

items.requirement: Eyewash units must deliver tepid flushing fluid (60-100°F / 15.5-37.8°C) at a minimum flow rate of 0.4 gallons per minute for at least 15 minutes. Safety showers must deliver tepid water at a minimum of 20 gallons per minute for at least 15 minutes. Gravity-fed units and self-contained units must have sufficient fluid capacity.
items.evidence_protocol: Activate 10% of eyewash units (minimum all units if fewer than 5) during the audit — this is the weekly activation test. Confirm: the unit activates in 1 second or less; both eyewash heads deliver a controlled, gentle flow simultaneously; the water temperature is tepid (not scalding, not freezing — use a thermometer if possible); flow continues for 15 minutes without manual intervention. Note units that cannot sustain 15-minute flow.

items.requirement: Emergency eyewash and shower equipment must be activated weekly to verify proper operation and to flush the supply line. Annual inspections must be performed by a qualified person. Self-contained units must be checked to ensure adequate fluid supply and the fluid is not contaminated.
items.evidence_protocol: Request weekly activation logs for 100% of eyewash and shower units for the past 3 months. Confirm: weekly activations are documented with date, unit location, and person performing the activation; units that failed the weekly test were taken out of service until repaired; annual inspection records are on file for each unit.

items.requirement: Employees who may be exposed to corrosive materials must be trained on the location and proper use of emergency eyewash and shower equipment and on the 15-minute flushing requirement.
items.evidence_protocol: Interview 10% of employees who work with corrosive chemicals (minimum 5). Ask each: where is the nearest eyewash station? What do you do if you splash chemical in your eye? How long must you flush? The 15-minute flushing requirement is frequently unknown — employees often stop flushing after a few seconds. Also confirm employees know NOT to rub their eyes and to seek medical attention after flushing.

items.requirement: Cutting and welding operations must be authorized by a designated management representative. The authorizing person must inspect the area before work begins, confirm fire prevention measures are in place, and ensure work is only performed in authorized locations.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the facility's hot work permit program. Confirm: a written hot work permit program exists and is being used; permits are issued before work begins (not after); the permit requires area inspection before and after the work; the permit identifies fire prevention measures in place (flammable materials removed or covered, fire watch assigned, fire extinguisher staged). Pull 10% of permits from the past 3 months (minimum 5) and confirm all required fields are completed.

items.requirement: Fire watchers must be assigned whenever welding or cutting is performed near combustible materials that cannot be moved. The fire watcher must maintain watch during and for at least 30 minutes after welding/cutting is complete. Fire watchers must be trained in use of fire extinguishing equipment and in activating the facility alarm.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask whether fire watches are required in the hot work permit program. Confirm fire watch requirements are triggered by the permit conditions checklist. Interview a fire watcher (or a welder who has had a fire watch): confirm the 30-minute post-work watch requirement is known and followed. Request training records for designated fire watchers.

items.requirement: Welding, cutting, or heating must not be performed on or near containers that held flammable substances, on drums or tanks with unknown contents, in areas with explosive atmospheres, or on pipes or systems that contain or carried flammable gases or liquids — unless specific cleaning and testing procedures have been completed.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask the maintenance manager and welders: 'What is the procedure before welding on a tank, drum, or pipe that previously contained a flammable or unknown substance?' Confirm a documented cleaning and testing procedure exists (purging, blanking, atmospheric testing). Review any hot work permits for work on piping or vessels — confirm tank/vessel cleaning certification was attached when required.

items.requirement: Suitable fire extinguishing equipment must be maintained for immediate use whenever welding or cutting is performed. Ducts, conveyor systems, and other equipment that might carry sparks or slag must be protected or shut down before welding begins.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe an active welding or cutting operation, or walk to a designated welding area. Confirm: a fire extinguisher is present and within the immediate area (not across the building); the extinguisher is appropriate for the likely fire class; any conveyor belts, pneumatic conveyors, or ductwork near the work area are shut down or protected during welding. Ask the welder where the nearest fire extinguisher is — they should know without looking.

items.requirement: Welders and helpers must be equipped with proper eye and face protection. Filter lens shade numbers must be appropriate for the welding or cutting process. Appropriate shades: SMAW 60-160A = shade 10; SMAW 160-250A = shade 12; MIG = shade 10-13; gas welding/brazing = shade 4-8; oxygen cutting = shade 3-6.
items.evidence_protocol: Inspect the PPE worn by or available to welders in 10% of welding/cutting areas (minimum 3 areas). Confirm: welding helmets with appropriate filter lenses are present; the shade number matches the process being performed; auto-darkening helmets meet ANSI Z87.1 and are tested for proper darkening; additional helpers or nearby workers are using flash goggles or shade 2-3 spectacles; welding areas are screened to protect adjacent employees.

items.requirement: Protective clothing must be provided and used during welding and cutting operations. Clothing must protect against sparks, spatter, and radiant heat. Combustible or synthetic fabrics must not be worn during welding operations without protective outerwear.
items.evidence_protocol: Observe welders in 10% of active welding areas (minimum 3 areas). Confirm: welding jackets or leather sleeves are worn; pants have no cuffs (cuffs catch sparks); leather boots or high-top footwear is worn; gloves appropriate for the operation are used; shirts are not made of synthetic fabrics without FR protective outerwear over them.

items.requirement: Local exhaust ventilation or general ventilation must be provided to keep concentrations of welding fumes, gases, and mists below the applicable PEL/TLV. Welding in confined spaces requires special ventilation attention and atmospheric monitoring.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of welding and cutting areas (minimum 3 areas). Confirm: local exhaust ventilation (LEV) is present at welding stations where fumes could accumulate; LEV hoods are positioned close to the source and operating; general ventilation is adequate in larger areas. For any welding performed in or near confined spaces — confirm atmospheric monitoring is conducted before and during welding. Ask whether any employees have complained of headaches, dizziness, or eye/throat irritation during welding — these are symptoms of inadequate ventilation.

items.requirement: Materials used in cutting, welding, and brazing must be included in the employer's Hazard Communication program and must be properly labeled. Welding consumables (rods, wire, flux, gases) must have SDSs available.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm SDSs are available for 10% of welding consumables in use (minimum 5 SDSs — e.g., welding rods, flux, shielding gas, anti-spatter spray). Confirm SDSs are in the 16-section GHS format and accessible in the welding area. Review the HazCom chemical inventory — confirm welding consumables are included.

items.requirement: Compressed gas cylinders used for welding and cutting must be stored, handled, and used safely. Oxygen and fuel gas cylinders must be stored separately. Cylinders must be secured, capped when not in use, and used through listed regulators only.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of gas welding/cutting stations and cylinder storage areas (minimum 3 locations). Confirm: oxygen and fuel gas (acetylene, propane) are stored at least 20 feet apart or separated by a 5-foot fire-rated noncombustible partition; cylinders are secured vertically with a chain or strap; valve caps are on all cylinders not in immediate use; regulators are attached only to cylinders with compatible fittings; cylinders are not used as an electrical ground.

items.requirement: Acetylene must not be used at pressures exceeding 15 psig. Acetylene hoses must not be interchanged with oxygen hoses. Hose connections must be leak-tested before use with soapy water or an approved leak detection solution — not with open flame.
items.evidence_protocol: Ask welders who use oxy-acetylene equipment: 'What is the maximum working pressure for acetylene?' (Answer: 15 psig). Inspect 10% of oxy-acetylene setups (minimum 3 setups): confirm regulators show working pressures at or below 15 psig for acetylene; hoses are properly color-coded (red for fuel gas, green for oxygen, or confirm labeling); no hose-to-hose adapters are in use that could cross-connect oxygen and fuel gas; hose connections are checked for leaks with soapy water (never an open flame).

items.requirement: Employers whose employees have occupational exposure (reasonably anticipated contact with blood or other potentially infectious materials — OPIM) must prepare a written Exposure Control Plan (ECP). The ECP must be accessible to employees, reviewed and updated at least annually, and updated whenever new tasks, procedures, or positions are created that affect occupational exposure.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm whether any employees at this facility have occupational exposure (first aid responders, medical personnel, employees who may provide emergency care). If yes: request the written ECP; confirm it includes an exposure determination (job classifications and tasks with occupational exposure); confirm the ECP was reviewed within the past 12 months; confirm it includes the schedule for implementing each provision of the bloodborne pathogens standard.

items.requirement: Engineering controls (sharps disposal containers, self-sheathing needles, safer medical devices) and work practice controls must be established and used to eliminate or minimize employee exposure. Engineering controls must be examined and maintained on a regular schedule.
items.evidence_protocol: If applicable to this facility: confirm sharps containers are present in all areas where sharps are used or generated; containers are puncture-resistant, leak-proof, and labeled with the biohazard symbol; containers are replaced when 3/4 full (not overflowing); no recapping of used needles by two-handed method. Confirm hand washing facilities are immediately accessible after any potential exposure.

items.requirement: Personal protective equipment (gloves, gowns, face shields, eye protection, resuscitation devices) must be provided at no cost to employees with occupational exposure and must be available in appropriate sizes.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the type and quantity of BBP PPE available to designated first aid responders. Confirm: latex-free alternatives are available for employees with latex allergies; resuscitation devices (face shields, bag-valve masks) are available to first aid providers; PPE is accessible at all locations where first aid may be provided.

items.requirement: Hepatitis B vaccination must be made available to all employees who have occupational exposure, at no cost to the employee, after training and within 10 working days of initial assignment to tasks with occupational exposure.
items.evidence_protocol: Request Hepatitis B vaccination records for 100% of employees with occupational exposure (first aid responders). Confirm each employee was offered the vaccine series within 10 working days of assignment to their first aid role. If an employee declined, confirm a signed declination form is on file. Confirm the employer paid for the vaccine.

items.requirement: Employers must provide post-exposure evaluation and follow-up at no cost to any employee who experiences an exposure incident. The follow-up must include documentation of the route of exposure, identification of the source individual, collection and testing of the exposed employee's blood, post-exposure prophylaxis, counseling, and evaluation of reported illnesses.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the post-exposure follow-up procedure. Confirm: a documented procedure exists for responding to an exposure incident; the procedure identifies the healthcare facility for post-exposure evaluation; the employer pays for all evaluation and follow-up. Ask the safety manager or first aid coordinator to walk through what they would do immediately after a first aid provider experienced a needlestick — the procedure should be known without referencing a manual.

items.requirement: Training must be provided to employees with occupational exposure at initial assignment and annually thereafter. Training must include explanation of the ECP, methods to recognize tasks that involve exposure, use of engineering controls and PPE, hepatitis B vaccine information, post-exposure procedures, and signs and labels.
items.evidence_protocol: Request BBP training records for 100% of employees with occupational exposure. Confirm: initial training was provided before first exposure; annual retraining records exist; training was conducted by or under the supervision of a person knowledgeable in the subject matter. Interview a designated first aid responder: ask what they do if they are splashed with blood, whether the hepatitis B vaccine was offered to them, and where the PPE is kept.

items.requirement: Medical records for each employee with occupational exposure must be maintained, including hepatitis B vaccination records, post-exposure follow-up documentation, and a copy of each employee's healthcare professional's written opinion. Training records must be maintained for 3 years.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm medical and training records are maintained and confidential. Confirm medical records are retained for the duration of employment plus 30 years (per 29 CFR 1910.1020). Confirm training records are retained for 3 years from the training date. Confirm records are not shared without employee consent (medical privacy).

items.requirement: Permanent places of employment must be kept clean. Floors must be cleaned regularly and kept free of liquid, refuse, and spills. Waste must be disposed of regularly to avoid health hazards.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of production, maintenance, break room, and restroom areas (minimum 5 areas). Document sanitation conditions: debris or trash accumulations; liquid spills or standing water; evidence of pest activity; food or beverages in non-designated areas. Confirm a written housekeeping program or cleaning schedule exists and is being followed.

items.requirement: A sanitary potable water supply must be provided for drinking, washing, cooking, and washing food. The supply must be from an approved source. Shared drinking cups or non-single-use drinking utensils are prohibited.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the potable water supply is from an approved source (municipal water system or tested well). Walk to drinking water stations in 10% of work areas (minimum 3 locations): confirm dispensers are clean; no shared cups or community dipping cups are in use; drinking fountains are clean and operational; water coolers/dispensers are maintained and cleaned regularly.

items.requirement: Separate and adequate toilet facilities must be provided. Minimum requirements: 1 toilet for 1-15 employees; 2 toilets for 16-35 employees; 3 toilets for 36-55 employees; 4 for 56-80; 5 for 81-110; 6 for 111-150; 1 additional for each additional 40 employees. Facilities must be maintained in a clean, sanitary condition.
items.evidence_protocol: Count the total number of toilet facilities (men's and women's combined) available to employees and compare to the workforce size using the table above. Walk to 10% of restroom facilities (minimum 2). Confirm: facilities are clean; hand washing soap, water, and drying means (paper towels or air dryer) are present; facilities are in working order; no urinals are inoperable.

items.requirement: Washing facilities must be provided near toilet facilities and in any area where employees are exposed to skin-contaminating or skin-absorbing substances. Facilities must include adequate supplies of soap and single-use towels or warm air blowers.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of handwashing stations in production areas, near restrooms, and near first aid locations (minimum 5 stations). Confirm: soap is present and dispensed properly; drying means (paper towels, air dryer) are available; hot and cold running water is provided; eyewash stations in chemical areas are distinct from handwashing facilities and clearly marked.

items.requirement: Food and beverages must not be consumed or stored in areas exposed to toxic or harmful materials. Change rooms must be provided where employees wear contaminated work clothing and the employer requires removal of clothing before leaving. Work clothing provided by the employer must be laundered by the employer, not taken home.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of production areas and confirm no food, beverages, or personal items are stored or consumed in areas where chemical or biological exposures exist. If contaminated work clothing is used: confirm change rooms are provided with separate storage for clean and contaminated clothing; confirm the employer's process for laundering contaminated clothing does not require employees to take it home.

items.requirement: Physical hazards must be marked using specific colors for identification: RED — fire protection equipment, danger signs, emergency stop; ORANGE — dangerous parts of machines, energized electrical equipment; YELLOW — caution, physical hazards, striking hazards, unstable surfaces; GREEN — safety/first aid; BLUE — information/caution against starting equipment under repair; FLUORESCENT ORANGE/ORANGE-RED — biological hazards.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of areas with physical hazards (minimum 5 areas). Confirm color coding is consistent and correct: fire extinguishers marked in red; machine guards and nip points in orange; floor markings for caution areas in yellow; first aid kits and eye wash stations in green; lockout tags in appropriate colors. Note any non-standard color coding that could cause confusion.

items.requirement: Accident prevention signs and tags must meet ANSI Z535.2 (signs) and Z535.5 (tags) design requirements. Signs must use appropriate signal words: DANGER (immediate hazard causing death or serious injury); WARNING (hazard that could cause death or serious injury); CAUTION (minor injury hazard); NOTICE (general information). Tags must contain a signal word and a major message describing the hazard.
items.evidence_protocol: Walk to 10% of hazard-identified areas (minimum 5 areas). Confirm: sign signal words match the severity of the hazard they describe (a CAUTION sign on a fatal fall hazard is deficient — must be DANGER); signs are posted where visible before reaching the hazard; lockout/tagout tags contain both a signal word and a description of the hazard; signs are not defaced, faded, or obscured.
